130320.ques: For the I'm a Princess music video, does the Cone on top of the stack of 3D shapes from the start (and end) refer to both Princess Party hats and Dunce hats, and is that the reason for the ending of "I'm a Dunce?"
130320.answ: the song audio was completed before the video was started, so there's no chance that the video influenced the audio. however, it's possible that the audio influenced the choices made in the video. Looking at the first scene, the important thing to remember about this scene is that i was basically a total beginniner in the 3D environment i was using at the time. so i probably had no idea if it was going to work. but then i put some lights in, then i was hooked

130321.ques: In perfect the characters mention several times they are going to go somewhere else but they never do. Was there originally a plan to have the characters go somewhere else or was it always the intention to have the characters remain in one place?
130321.answ: writers often talk about the advantages and disadvantages of 'outlining' or planning out your plot before you write. the more i consider this issue, the more and more eagerly i throw myself onto the no-outline bandwagon. That means at each moment when i'm writing the story, i have just as no idea what's going to happen next as you the first-time viewer. i could rave through the ceiling trying to explain all the advantages i find in doing it this way. most importantly it ensurees that the writer and viewer are always in the same space. but anyway, this video in particular is a celebration of the no-outline method, so it's plan-free. are they going somewhere? they certainly can, the option is always open, so that's probably why the possibility comes up from time to time. you can be sure that any time someone asks if they are going somewhere, i the writer did not know the answer yet at that time

130334.ques: I have a lot of ideas for projects/things I can work on, do you have any advice about keeping yourself focused enough to make progress on a few at a time without getting distracted or overwhelmed by the possibilities?
130334.answ: basically what may happen as this: you struggle to figure it out, you still can't figure it out. you struggle to figure it out, you still can't figure it out. you want desperately to figure it out, your desire to figure it out increases, you still can't figure it out. it goes on and on like this, until eventually, you start to actually realize 'i may never get it. i may never figure this out. ive been trying so long and it still hasnt worked, i actually am starting to think it might never work.' But as you sink lower into desperation, simpler possibilities become more and more exciting. So ultimately if you sink down low enough, heck even just the idea of doing each project one at a time in alphabetical order, that suddenly seems incredibly exciting because in a world where you were scared that there are no possibilities, there now actually is one

130442.ques: Hi, I'm the guy that has problems with the perfectionism. You asked If perfect even is a good quality, and this is a valid question. I obviously know that mistakes and so on make the song better. Quantization ruins the groove and mistakes enhance the finished artwork to a level a "perfect" song never could achieve. Like the missing arms of the venus of milow. Obviously a mistake, but makes the statue sooo much better Anyway, I want to reformulate the question a bit, because this is not exactly what I was trying to ask. It's not that the song has to be perfect but I have to be satisified 100% with my Song to release it. But you probably know that we artist get sick of our songs bc we listen to it so much. So my question is: Are you always 100% satisified and a fan of your stuff at the time of release or in production? Or do you think "ahh it sounds bad but I release it anyways" and if so, how can you release stuff that you aren't 100% satisfied with? This is I think much more what I was trying to ask.
130442.answ: i have to convince myself that i am, even if just for one moment. otherwise i'd never get out of there if you get scared to release it, then just don't release anything and wait for that to drive you so ridiculously insane that you'll be ready to strike a deal with yourself

130458.ques: When you listen to music, as a professional musician yourself, do you think you pick up on some specific details about the way the music was composed or produced that non-musicians wouldn't necessarily pick up on? Would you say that this is more of a blessing, or a curse, or both?
130458.answ: it comes full circle. in the first few years of music study, musicians may find themselves noticing things in a different way, but then you might spend subsequent years trying to course correct. then at that point you may feel you are seeing things in more 'deeper' or 'genuine' ways than younger musicians might be seeing them, ..... and then just when you think you've really got some good balanced perspective, you hear the opinion of a non-musician and it turns out that what they think they like about the music is actually the video, and what they think they like about the video is actually the music

Almost the kind of thing that would make you want to skip this page and pick another, more fruitful page. but i was not to fall for that so easily. by then i had already been well trained in one of the first ground rules of creativity, which is that garbage can be turned into fruit, quite handily in fact

130644.ques: do you think you've ever made art that could be considered "avantgarde"? what do you consider to be avantgarde?
130644.answ: in my view, here's where the 'avantgarde' comes into play. It's when you're bursting with creative desire but for one reason or another you feel like a hopeless amateur in whatever field you're working in. you feel technically under qualified and under-experienced, for some reason. and of course the only way to get good is to start getting some practice, but you're so concerned that the first few hundred steps you take will seem horribly lacking and underprofessional. and so you get to work, and blame the avant garde. under the guise of 'avant garde', the low-brow has a chance to immediately become high-brow. trash can go on display in a museum. heck, if you can't get into a normal museum, make an avant garde museum, which can of course be made anywhere. Maybe that sounds too easy, or trivial, but, i can assure you: the avant garde is very real, it's rock solid, and it can be a real life saver when you need it

130648.ques: Hey, starting a game development project, and I've been wondering which I should build first: a good story that I put mechanics onto, or a solid bit of gameplay that I weave a story into. I already know what I want the story and gameplay to each be about, but the different processes feel like they'll end up with wildly different results. Obviously, I'm not asking you to choose for me, but do you have any advice on how to choose a process when they're so different?
130648.answ: honestly both ways are great, but you are probably right that they have different results. the only way to know is to try both ways. I'm not suggesting you try both for this project, but i am probably suggesting you try it one way for this project and the other way for another project. it doesn't matter which order you try them, and you can almost be sure that there's probably an alternate version of you in a different reality who did it in the opposite order. could even be fun to pretend that's you the first time around

130667.ques: oh my gosh i just have to share this with you: i was working on a video and i was debating 16:9 and 3:4, and took note of the common resolution number 1440. then i was synchronizing an animation to music and needed something to spin at 180 degrees every ~4 frames to be a 16th note in length per revolution - and needed it to spin for eight (8) 16ths notes which let me to this same number i was working with earlier when calculating: [total rotation needed for 180 degrees/16th over eight (8) 16th's] or 8 * 180 = 1440(degrees) and i was quite taken aback upon reading that from the calculator. and now i wonder why resolutions use these circular numbers and also why do circles. couldnt # of degrees per 1 rotation be based on any number besides 360? wait is it a CIRCLES RESOLUTION? im glad you were here for this unplanned epiphany thanks for being someone to talk about this to
130667.answ: the reason 360 is a good number for a circle is because it divides well. you can divide by 2, 3, 4, 5, or 6 and still have a whole number. very useful. screen resolutions probably also have usefulness in being well dividable, for example if you want to display something at half size, or a third size. 1440 can also be neatly divided by 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6. so it's not the hugeset coincidence that you ran into 1440 twice in this process.

130672.ques: Ive been working on relearning the ancient practice of creating Gods. A god is a being that exists in the mind of a collective. The gods existence arises from the shared agreement that they exist. Through the plecebo effect, actions taken by the Gods become real to those with faith. Once those who beleive reach critical mass, the God begins to grow powerful. If a human can fit the description of the deity, and convince the faithful he has mantled it. That human now has the power to control the plecebo effect in their followers and can cause miracles like healing wounds that wouldnt heal. All by plecebo effect. What are your thoughts on this?
130672.answ: i see exactly what you're getting at, but i view all this as sort of the problem that happens when you try to pin it down to one namable thing or force. truly what 'God'(s) are based on is a much deeper inherent property of the universe, but usually any attempt to put it in a box (including mine, right here with this explanation) will result in its immediate corruption. and also, i'll say i find it totally appropriate that this is such a central challenge that we as earthlings face

130779.ques: hey bill, i've come up with an interpretation for a lyric, and i'm not gonna if it's right 'right interpretation' but rather if you think it's reasonably sound. in got some money, there's a part that's like some call it 4.95, some call if 3.99, some call it a billion and is that eluding to how in super economically stable countries, they try to make their dollar the strongest even maybe by a super small amount, and in smaller maybe 3rd world countries it can have a super bad economy and their currency isn't as much. then the billion took a lot longer to sing than the other two, but they're all separate statements, equal to each other, but the billion lasts longer for the same value, which canbe like how there are a lot more poorer countries out in the world that could use help, but they're mentioned a lot less than 'rich' countries. That would affect like almost only people in the rich, because they're the only ones that can watch it, like you and me, who think and care about big 'important' countries that are rich and prosperous. perhaps i'm just talking absolute nonsense
130779.answ: basically what happens is, if i come up with a line and it goes a certain way, i get a deep electrified feeling of excitement over everything about how it goes. the words, the music, and all the millions of deep things that it seems to convey and mean, simultaneously. something that gets me deeply and profoundly high, on a mental and emotional and spiritual level simultaneously, plus a plethora of other new levels i may not even have a name for. When a line does this for me, it's a keeper. this may or may not be the way anyone else feels about the lines, but this is how i songwrite. it's basically the only method i have

130780.ques: I bet when you hear a new song you hear very different things in it than I would. Do you notice chords, chord progressions, form, instrumentation, percussion, etc? What do you listen for in a new song? I pretty much just hear the general vibe and the melody, and whether it's pop, classical, country, etc. unless something else really stands out. I think it's interesting that we could be listening to the same music, but hear very different things. It would be fun to listen to you describe what you hear in real time, so maybe I could learn to hear those things, too.
130780.answ: sure, but what's most important to underline, above all, is that anything can happen i just let the song play, whilst i can hear it. then just see what happens. preparing to be surprised, on the deepest levels if possible deep down, whenever we talk about music (you, me, or anyone), really we're just making connections between what we think happened in the song, and anything else we may be familiar with. there's no limit to the different kinds of things that can be envoked in such a conversation. the more the merrier

130919.ques: i've been guitaring and whatnot for a couple years now and i got the idea to make a shitty little EP just cause i want to play music and get music out there. i have a tendency to procrastinate indefinitely if i'm not actually making steps towards something. but 15 minutes of trying to figure out how you record stuff has totally zapped me of any desire to do it. even like, buying mics, i don't know what a compressor is. it's an entire new skillset (and money sink) i wasn't prepared for. penny for your thoughts
130919.answ: yes i give this thought all the time. at the risk of boring some people to death who always hear me give the same advice, i will say that the feeling you are having is exactly what happens when you start too big. now you may be saying 'but wait, i didnt think i was starting that big', well, too bad, it's still too big for you at this moment. and so youve been knocked on the ground. The answer to this is extremely simple, and it's just to start smaller. For example, you say 'i dont know what a compressor is.' Ok, great. then dont use it right now. Honestly, it's a huge shame that you have even heard of a 'compressor' right now, because all it's doing for you is bringing you some sense of 'obligation' to do something youve never even identified, never consciously needed or wanted, means nothing to you at this time. which is precisely why you had no energy to think about it. So anyway, back to a mic, because yes you do want make music that can be stored in a bottle and played back at a different time (i believe that was your original desire.) This is a lot like going up a staircase. when you approach the stairs for the first time, are you saying "Which step SHOULD i go to? which is the best and most professional step, to step on?" and then you get overwhelmed and pass out into a depressed nap. but what you should have asked instead is ARE ANY OF THE STEPS STEPPABLE, FROM THE GROUND. are any of the steps right next to the ground where i can step on it. the answer is yes and that would of course be step number 1 for you. Anyway, back to mics, dont buy a mic, the golden rule to start, is of course 'use the one you already have.' Yes, if you really dont have any microphone device, then go to the nearest store and buy any mic, but the thing is i'm sure you actually do already have a microphone device. almost any device that can type something into this question box probably has a mic on it. in contrast to that horrible dreadful feeling of confusion and guilt, i think you may find that the idea of starting your musical journey using only whatever's already right there with you on your deserted island, is just absolutely romantic. you can take your pet volley ball along

130961.ques: Sorry this is a long one but I'll try to keep it broad. I make videos and I often hear that you should make content for yourself, and if you build it they will come. I do find that my videos that have broader appeal tend to perform better (on the tiny scale that my videos "perform" at all) but it seems that the only ideas I can consistently think of are the more esoteric "for me" ideas. should i just stick to my guns and keep making stuff for myself?
130961.answ: i guess you just have to ask yourself whether you are a leader or a follower. i remember actually one time i saw someone post a message to their audience asking 'what kind of content would you like me to make' and i instantly realized how empty that looks for an audience. they're here for you to take them somewhere. heck, sometimes they dont even know how to articulate that, they may clamour 'sequel this' or 'reboot that' but they dont know what theyre talking about. they want to be taken somewhere, and you are here to dream up somewhere new to take them. if they knew exactly what that was then they wouldnt need you

131063.ques: do your creative ideas come naturally to you more often than not? in any case, what's your brainstorm process like when you want an idea on the spot?
131063.answ: technically the only way to get a creative idea is naturally. but it's so intoxicating that almost no one can resist the temptation to still try additionally; which of course ruins it sometimes the only way to get relaxed is to just do bad ideas on purpose just to take the pressure off. the paradox is that of course this often immediately launches it, unexpectedly. but then that works so incredibly good that that of course becomes so intoxicating that you again cannot resist temptation to tryhard to somehow repeat the miracle. and then of course it doesnt work. in which case it once again becomes a good idea to do actual bad ones for a while because it's still the only way to take the pressure off

131065.answ: you're way ahead of me. i dont know what all this talk is about 'polishing', but i've never even heard of that. never even gotten close to getting anywhere near that far. if i can EVER get anything ANY good, that's considered a very rare and grade-A first class miracle, which under normal circumstances cannot be relied upon or taken for granted

131142.ques: Again, thank you for not posting a lot of opinions about potentially divisive political and social topics. I'm so weary of all the polarized arguments in our society, and I love that I can just enjoy your music and come to your website without having to think about whether we see eye to eye on issues, which could distract from the wonder of your art. It's a relief not to have to go there. Do you intentionally avoid those types of topics?
131142.answ: i am often reminded of the trauma of what it might be like to ride in a car with a brother and sister who hate each other. they each make a pretty convincing (and terrifying) argument of why the other one is evil. what do you do? what can you even think? it's a total mindfuck. deep down i know that the whole truth is peaceful as the sun is bright, but what language do you even use to explain that, given that everyone to your left and right have corrupted all communication, in the ongoing process of mindraping each other

131151.ques: bill can you explain what chord progression is i've been struggling with understanding it
131151.answ: maybe we should start here: do you know what a chord is. and, have you ever heard a song. beyond that, the phrase 'chord progression' literally means nothing, and i dont actually like the phrase because it tricks so many people like you into thinking it's something much more than it is. heck, i think it tricked me once, long ago. before i realized it's just chords. It's like if people went around talking about something called a 'clothing millieu' and youre like, what's that, what are the requirements for a millieu, but no, fuck it, it just means what clothes you're wearing. fancy word to distract

131160.ques: bill your song 'i'm a princess' raises real questions about the true meaning of royal lineage. the princess is evidently moving towards better things despite her (his?) paranoia about the neighbours (symbolising the working class) so it makes me wonder about the self-reinforced legitimacy of authority. then of course the conclusion completely brings down our understanding of princesshood and tells us that it was an illusion all along, that we allowed the fantasy of royalty to exist, it was just written in the fine print that it was a construction. so are you really a princess?

131162.ques: Why do siren noises change as they get closer then then change back once farther away?
131162.answ: it's not just siren noises, it's any noise that's moving fast toward you, or away from you. they are higher than normal when they are coming fast toward you, and they are lower than normal when they are going fast away. that's because the sound waves get bunched up when it's going toward you, and the sound waves get spread out when going away. i am not the first person to describe this effect, nor is my name Doppler

131200.ques: when you finish writing a song but haven't yet recorded it, to which degree do you know how it's going to sound like, compared to when it's fully finalized? have you ever ended up with a wildly different result than what you were expecting while writing?
131200.answ: in some ways it's better and in some ways it's worse. but what's always firm before the production is everything the vocal sings and says, and how the chords work. and the exact tempo and the exact roadmap (exactly when it goes to which part, and for how long, exactly the number of bars everywhere. all the way to the end. no open questions with that). and by extension the exact run-time would be known. On the other hand, what's almost 0% known, before production, would be anything textural, or any instrument or sonic choices, and the specific ways those instruments and sounds coalesce to deliver the song content and support the vocal. those issues are all wide open

131218.ques: my dad always said a couple is 2, a few is 3-6 and several is 7+. I ignored most everything my parents ever said to me but for some reason I internalized that hard. even if it's definitely different from what most people mean by 'several'
131218.answ: well, try doing something for 4 days and see if that feels like several days to you. on paper, the word 'several' may look like 'seven', but go and see what the experience counts for. also this raises an interesting issue which is 'several' things are likely to be part of a sequence. if you have 7 items all at once, i almost think that might be called a 'bunch'. but it also depends on the size of the item and what the reasonable quantities are. for example if you had some grains of rice in a bag and you poured about 23 grains onto a plate, you can easily call that a few. it wouldn't really feel like 'several' unless you took them out one at a time

131243.ques: I often times get mildly annoyed when certain songs set themselves up to "transcend" but then... they don't end up transcending. What I mean by transcending is difficult to put in to words, it's something like when a comedian is telling an elaborate joke, the crowd is already dying with laughter BUT THEN he goes even further that noone could've anticipated - like that but just in musical form. Whenever that happens, I often just pause the song and imagine in my mind how I would transcend the song, and then I end up wondering why the artist didn't transcend it like that, or why they didn't transcend it at all. Can you relate to some of this mild annoyance?
131243.answ: maybe they aren't capable of doing it like you could do it. or, maybe they transcended in a different way and you just didn't follow it. or, maybe they did not actually think they were 'setting themselves up to transcend.' so many different ways for this to play out, but the underlying factor is simply that we're not all on the exact same trip at each given time. it's kind of a miracle that music ever even connects to anyone at all. which is why i am hesitant to place blame when it doesnt, even if the 'problem' may seem so clear to me at a certain point in time. the best thing here is probably to save it for your own work. and actually the fact that you're feeling this critique, this can be seen as a mounting desire for you to make your own music

131386.ques: Hey Bill, on some days I wake up and have this weird sense of confusion. Like I can have plenty of really good days in a row where everything works fine, I have energy, my routine is working, my project is going well and I'm just overall confident. And then sometimes the next day, completely out of nowhere my brain is like doubting everything and giving me all sorts of reasons why things aren't gonna work out and I'm just... confused? Have you dealt with this before and do you maybe have a solution to this?
131386.answ: this will always happen from time to time, and there's no way around it, but here's why you should be glad: without this part of the cycle, the other parts wouldn't be any good. they literally would not be defined as anything good, in comparison to anything. no matter what ocean wave you have, there is a downside to the wave. the only way to avoid the downside is to avoid the entire wave altogether, thus also avoiding the upside

131441.ques: hi bill, i read your answer to a question about chord progressions a few weeks ago where you said you don't like that phrase, since it tricks people into thinking it's something much more than it is. is there another way or term you think is more useful to understand patterns of chords? i'm very curious about how you think about this, your music makes me feel uniquely groovy, and i think the chords you choose and the way you use/break patterns are a big reason why i enjoy it so much
131441.answ: there's no way to describe what chords do. the more songs you get to know, the more things you try out for yourself, the more familiar (and curious!) you will continue to get, and, just like recognizing faces, you'll be fully equipped to play the game to the fullest but you still will never be able to pin down exactly what works and why. and this becomes a more and more shocking revelation the more experienced you get, because, despite all that experience, you're thinking "how could i, the expert, still not be able to pin this down?" until you realize, ..... the music, just like a mischievous child, actually takes personal thrills in unpindownableness. and in fact so does the listener

131610.ques: Does perfection exist in any context, or is it a delusion? I struggle to find the balance between doing something I'm proud of and doing something ever at all before I die, do you have any advice?
131610.answ: an exact question or assignment can have an exact answer. for example, the perfect answer to 1+1 is probably 2. but if the goal is something more ellusive, such as, create joy and happiness, then it's impossible to predict what methods will have what result. and it would be completely impossible to organize or rate the qualities of those possible outcomes starting doing things will get you both. not starting will get you neither. there should be no debate here, you'll just need some patience, and perhaps to embrace the avant garde a little just at the start

131714.ques: I'm kind of confused. No pressure to help, but if you could I'd appreciate it! Is a minor seventh chord - we can use Gm7 as an example - supposed to be read as a minor chord, with a minor seventh (G Bb D F), or is it a G chord, with a minor seventh (G B D F)? Thank you and have a great day!
131714.answ: a chord called "G minor 7", often written as "Gmin7", or "G-7", or "Gmi7", will most importantly include notes Bb and F, then less crucial but likely containing note D, and in the bass will be note G. the other chord you mentioned (with note B instead of Bb) will be known as 'G7'

131725.answ: i've never heard of that specific effect, but keyboards often have a lot of stuff that works for non-players. if you are a player (keyboard player, piano player, musician, someone who likes to play all your own notes) then you'll find you often need to tweak the sounds to simplify them so you can actually get your moneys worth out of the actual notes you played. Like imagine if your letter keyboard had full sentences every time you type one letter. Handy if you don't know how to type your own sentences, but if you do know how to type your own sentences, then you're going to get more benefit out of something that will just put down the exact letters you type when you type them

131777.ques: Hey Bill! I am trying to write my own music and Im wondering what you would recommend I focus on learning next. I'm comfortable with 7th chords and I finally understand the difference between dominant 7s vs. Major and minor 7s. I know the major scale (do re me fa so la ti do), and I can use the chords/scale degrees (I ii iii IV V vi vii) to build progressions.. But I feel like when I write, I keep playing octaves and a 5th on the left hand and major or minor triads on the right. Now that I learned all major, minor and 7th chords in root position, everything I play as a roman numeral progression sounds so similar (and not very great since I don't know my inversions yet) I'm vaguely comfortable using diminished/augmented chords or some passing chords here and there and I'm familiar with raising the bass note a whole step up for a jazzy slash chord at the very end of a phrase... But other than a few things like that, my jazz vocabulary is pretty limited and I can't seem to figure out how you do all these complex key modulations. There has to be a better way than aimlessly watching a bunch of jazz music theory videos on YouTube, right? Should I grind out the inversions? Should I learn how to write in minor keys? If you have a textbook I could read or anything to point me in tne right direction, I'd really appreciate your help!
131777.answ: do the words. you remind me of someone who has studied cars for thousands of years but has never even seen a road. do the words now

131781.ques: what do you mean by "i'm an idiot." like, what kind of idtiot.
131781.answ: let's put this back in context here. someone asked me why i am bad at collaborating. the truth is that i usually don't like anyone else's ideas. but i thought that wasn't very nice to say, so i said something totally different instead ("i'm an idiot"). It may certainly be true, but it's not what it feels like. when i try to collaborate, actually, what it feels like is that i'm a total genius and the other person is an idiot. which, needless to say, is totally obnoxious and i would not want to subject anyone to that. that being said, i am still not ruling out the fact that i am a total idiot and just don't realize it. heck, i've seen that happen to plenty of people, there's no reason why it couldn't or shouldn't happen to me

131850.ques: When you write answers in other languages, do you tend to use a translator or you just know how to speak the language

131851.ques: how do you usually go about starting a new song? is there anything you do about Writer's Block specifically?
131851.answ: there are basically 2 ways a song is born. by accident, or on purpose. In neither case will that be at the same time that the rest of the song is written, but the initial idea comes about either by accident or on purpose. if it's by accident, for example, if it's in a dream, then there was no writer's block because i wasn't even expecting anything. all i have to do is write it down. but the alternative way that songs are born is on a deliberate session where i try to come up with new things. and basically the way to start the engines there is just start plowing forward and come up with as many things as i can. sometimes bad on purpose, just to get things rolling. then somewhere within the first 10, something comes alive and at least one of them always is good

131951.ques: I was in a language learning Discord server at some point, and someone brought up a really interesting practice method. First some context. Suppose person A speaks French and wants to learn English, and the reverse for person B. Sometimes these people get together for "language exchange", which in this case is having a conversation in French, and then the next will be in English, and they'd alternate like this at whatever frequency A and B agree upon. The problem is that people who engage in this practice are usually not yet familiar enough with the language to make non-trivial sentences themselves, which greatly limits how complex the conversations can get. So it's hard and somewhat boring. The idea is that since these people need more input in their target language, they should just have conversations where A speaks French and B replies in English. They both get natural input in their respective target languages, without being limited in what they want to say. This sounds great on paper, so I'd like to convince the entire universe to try it to see how well it holds up in practice. How would I go about this?
131951.answ: i already know that's a great method. and it sounds like you suspect it also. of course what i would suggest is not to go trying to convince the entire universe of something. if it's good, the universe will probably find out anyway. and if it's not, then they'll be pretty mad at you for trying to force this on them

132247.ques: What are some of your ideas catching techniques? For instance, say a song idea comes to me while I'm in the car or when I'm already rushing to get ready in the morning (which is when most of my song ideas come to me of course)
132247.answ: if you have no immediate way to write it down, start analyzing it in non-musical ways to remember the data. for example: quiz yourself about how many notes it has. quiz yourself about what tempo value it has. count the words. make up acronyms that match the first letters of the words. list out the note values. quiz yourself on how many 8th rests there are. these are basically just to familiarize yourself with the data from all angles, and it serves to just help you remember the information. also you can pretend you are dictating it to someone else to write down, who has no knowledge of music notation. so imagine you have to explain to them how to transcribe all the deetails. all these tricks should help you remember the correct original information accurately without it being carelessly mixed up with something else equally musical or plausible

132301.ques: when you try to find the prime factors while waiting in a line, are you doing this in your head???? how do you even go about doing that?????
132301.answ: yes i do it in my head, it's a casual empire of skill i've been quietly building over the past 15 years or so. to tell you every trick and technique might take 15 years, but, for example, let's say the number is 166747. i could tell you how to tell it's not divisible by 2, but you probably already know how to do that. i could tell you how to tell it's not divisible by 3, but, they probably already taught that one to you in school. i could tell you how to tell it's not divisible by 5, but you probably already know that also. they tend to teach you in school there's not really any trick to checking anything higher, so i will tell you how to check 7 (or anything). manipulate it by adding or subtracting multiples of 7 until you shrink it enough to recognize it. level it off to a zero at the end and drop the zero, because dividing by 10 never changes its 7-status. you can also divide by 2 if it ever becomes even, if you feel like it. 166747 --> 16674 ---> 1666 ---> 161. i already recognize it there, but if not then you'd subtract 21 and get 14. which you probably recognize. but then again, i'm not really sure where other people are at on this, because i've been thinking about numbers all my life. i'm perfectly familiar with 14 as a multiple of 7, but i have no idea if this is general common knowledge to other people. 1001, 501, 301, 203, 323, 216, 1024, all those numbers are just as familiar to me as 14, just because of how long i've been doing this, but i have no idea how far others have gone on this. so that's why i was able to stop when i hit 161, because i already recognize that as 23x7, and to get there i readily used 7, 14, and 56. this was me trying to give a short answer, are you proud of me

132559.ques: During a lecture, a composition teacher told us that when you're composing, there are various rules that you're made aware of in some way (this is mainly referring to Western music)-rules of harmony, of cadences, of structure, of arrangement, etc. and you have three ways to interact with these rules-you can either: a. rigorously follow them and let your musical creativity flow within the confines of these rules, b. allow yourself some freedom and flexibility with these rules by stretching, bending and occasionally breaking them as you see fit while still keeping them in mind (he symbolized this as making a sort of mutual compromise between you and these rules), and c. completely disregard these rules and make your own rules. Now, this is clumsily paraphrased, but you get the idea, I hope. I thought that that was an interesting way to think of it, but what do you think? Do you think any of those options correspond in some way to how you write music? If so, which one(s)?
132559.answ: the flexibility is a fine sentiment, but i think there's an elephant in the room. if you even try once to 'describe' one of these 'rules', it slips through your fingertips like gourmet slime butter. and you should never follow a 'rule' because you 'think' you 'should', only follow a rule because you adore the taste of gourmet slime butter. that being said, the flexibility spectrum of how much and how little, let's say, 'predictability' to employ, that is still very much in play, and can be dialed in, to taste

132562.ques: do you remember why you picked the word 'die' for the die video?
132562.answ: sure, keep in mind that was audio only first, years earlier. the reason is because i was following my own advice, that i now so frequently give to people. people say "how do i get into writing songs, i'm so stumped, i can't think of any ideas, i don't know what to do, i don't know what to say, too much doubt, too much pressure, i don't know how to do this", and i always tell them, if nothing else works (and often nothing else will), try to make the stupidest worst thing you can. whatever you think the 'rule' is for what a song is supposed to be, try doing precisely not that. just to see if you can get some excitement going. i yammer on and on relentlessly nowadays with this sort of writing advice, but i think you've found evidence that i've been through this before and the advice comes from real life experience

132609.ques: so why do you think chords resolve down by a fifth so well
132609.answ: yea but when you actually start using them, this description of "resolve down by a 5th" doesnt really describe what the chords are actually doing. i'm sure that's what they probably told you in the classroom, but if you take a minute and look at what's actually happening that's just not quite it. in my opinion if you want to demonstrate the most basic harmonic notion of 'resolve', i'd say play a C note with F note above, then keep the C note going and switch the F to E. this is especially important if you consider that this might be the only part people will hear, if they're not plugged in

132626.ques: With the assumption that we all grow and become better/more skillful with time, it is silly to record every idea you have. If you are not going to act on the thought right away, then it becomes worse and worse compared to any new idea you could have.

132656.ques: how did you learn sheet music i've been playing around with the piano until i got to a point where i believe i know what i'm doing but i've never bothered to learn the name of the keys and notes and chords and also never bothered to know what the hell sheet music is, it just looks like a mess of lines to me all i know when it comes to classifying a note (if i'm not doing it by ear, in my own way) is "do re mi fa so la si do" and i've seen people say "ti" instead of "si" so now i'm questioning whether i really know what i'm doing

132863.ques: I think it's interesting how the medium influences how music is written and presented. Like, pre-recorded music you had pieces of all sorts of lengths because there was no need to "fit" a piece of music onto a radio or record. Then with recordings you had a very pretermined "quantity" of music you can fit on there .. you started seeing the famous 3-5min long song for radio and 45's/singles, and maybe a full album, cassette or CD you can have somewhere around 30-45min of music. I think it's weird though that in the digital age, not much has changed since the early days of recorded music. With less people buying hard copies of music and internet/streaming becoming more common than radio...The digital age is more similar to the pre-recorded days in that you can have a piece of music last however long you want since you dont need to fit it into a physical medium... and yet, we still see a majority of songs (at least popular songs) fitting in that 3-5min range and albums (though not as popular as they once were, with many artists putting out singles instead of albums) are still typically in that 30-45min range when an artist does put out an album. Do you think streaming and stuff like that just hasn't been around long enough yet for popular music to start taking more liberties with song/album durations? Or is the 3-5min song 30-45min album just a sweet spot that we've hit that we'll stick to regardless of how the music is being released/listened to?
132863.answ: i think the sweet spots a little shorter, in general

132989.ques: I've sometimes heard other self-employed artists who make content for YouTube say that while they love their jobs, one thing that they kind of miss from having a "normal" job is that it makes it easier to have a good work-life balance-if you work a 9-to-5, in most cases, you have a certain amount of time in the day when you're on the clock, at the office (or wherever you work), working; but once your shift is over, you have time left over in the day to do other non-work-related activities, and go to bed at a fairly consistent time every day-therefore the day is clearly separated into time when you're working, and time when you aren't. But when you're your own boss and work from home or in a studio, there's not that same clear separation between time for working and time for doing other things, so it's easy to get so caught up in the work you're doing-because you're passionate about it-that it occupies the large majority of your day, which isn't inherently a bad thing, but that can lead to not having much time for anything else-such as going outside, hanging out with friends, or even sleeping-which can have long-term negative effects on your relationships and health. Can you relate to these struggles in any way? Do you have any advice for self-employed artists having difficulty maintaining a good work-life balance?
132989.answ: i've tried every different way, but i'm still not sure which one is 'best'. perhaps a rotation through different strategies over time is inevitable. that being said, plenty of cool permanent strategies and habits find themselves along the way, but these are absolutely so different for each person, different people can be stylistically polar opposites in their work patterns. it's an exciting journey, and even if i knew exactly how it was gonna turn out for you, i wouldn't want to spoil it

133024.ques: why is it that one evening i can pull a random song fragment from my list and get an entire approximate verse, prechorus, and chorus from it withing 5 minutes.... but then i sit down multiple times over the next few days for hours and get absolutely nowhere with it? the lyrics are all pretty approximate and don't really make much sense together (and not in the fun way - in the "this feels like it's trying to tell multiple different stories" kinda way) i'm very very very confident in my structural/melodic choices (all of which came from that brief flash of songwriting), but it just feels like it doesn't wanna do anything else now. it's like i spotted someone dancing out of the corner of my eye, i looked at them, and then they got scared and ran away or something. and of course, this definitely isn't the only song of mine that's stuck in this exact situation. any advice here would be appreciated, thanks!
133024.answ: the reason this songwriting thing is so unpredictable is because it's magic, but, here's what can help, in your situation. zoom out, zoom out. look at the big picture. let the whole history of this song flash in front of you. when did you first think of it? staying closer to how you got into it will also keep you close to how you're gonna get out of it. actually a really crazy trick which works for me pretty much every time is to start writing about the previous writing session. in the song. wild trick but i can't remember it ever not working

133108.ques: what is the point of nonsensical sayings like "when pigs fly"?
133108.answ: the point of that is to compare the unlikelyness of that to something else unlikely. For example: someone says "i am going to win the olympics next year". and the other person says sarcastically, "yeah sure, when pigs fly you'll win the olympics", by which they mean "NEVER" will that happen. another classic version of this is "when hell freezes over" with the implication that it will not. but let's be honest, those ones are both so stale that they feel meaningless (as you said), so you might as well make up a new one. maybe something like "when this bottle of honey reaches its expiration date" (honey does not usually have an expiration date)

133230.ques: So I dunno why but I like it when.. how do I explain this. So basically y'know how in the "I'm a princess" song you had this part where it transitioned from "There's got to be so much more" to "But I don't know what it's there for"? I don't know what it is or what it's called but I like it a lot when people do that. Could you maybe explain what it is by any chance? Thanks.
133230.answ: basically it's like when you go from one room to another but you specifically like the feeling of the door frame when your shoe glides over it as you go through the door. in that moment, the melody is jumping from one harmonic room to another, and sort of sliding its foot across the doorframe, just to feel the change between the two rooms. Or, what the heck, maybe you are just talking about rhythm, maybe you just like it when someone hits the e of 4. two people can watch the same movie and react to totally different things about them, so there is no way to be sure what you are into about that part, but this is great news regardless because i always considered that part a crappy re-write (of something worse before, but still a crappy re-write) so it is very validading to hear that it works for you

133286.ques: The choice to make the main hook in "i'm a princess" that synth lead (I think that's how to describe it) is so beautiful. Why is it so beautiful? Can you explain?
133286.answ: at a certain point many years ago when i was walking in the park i suddenly noticed something: handing off between lines of lyric and instrumental riffs is one of the strongest musical strategies you can have. you can make the words sound like they're singing about the riff, or you can make the riff sound like it's singing about the words. You hear a riff, then the words say what it means. Or, some words are sung, and then the riff explains how you feel about them that you couldn't explain in words. lyrics and non-lyrics are the perfect co-conspirators

133301.ques: If there are monday and tuesday videos, what are the video types for the other days of the week?
133301.answ: around 2014 i had a 5 day scheme: new song releases (audio only) were wednesday. adding a video to a song i'd already released would happen on tuesday and friday. a non-song video would be on monday and thursday. and there was one more distinction between those 4 video formats: use cameras/voice or no cameras/voice. monday and friday would be no camera/voice, and tuesday and thursday would be yes use cameras and voice. then later in 2015 i was doing just a 2-day schedule where it was only monday and thursday. the monday format remained the same, and the thursday format also remained the same except they were written differently because i improvised the part on camera and then wrote the rest around it. For a brief period of time in 2015 i think i was doing 'music school' videos on sunday. and then i think friday and saturday were sometimes a pair

133580.ques: Does "If this song ain't good enough I'll erase it" apply to all your songs, or just Here Comes the Sun?
133580.answ: what it actually means is, as youre listening to it, at that point, the song will now erase itself from the present moment, by ending. it's a song-ender. it doesnt mean you cant listen to the song again, but it just removes it from the list of songs that you are listening to at that moment. it's how the song copes with the fact that it is not good enough to go on forever, which is something that every song must confront at some point

133639.ques: thoughts on radically simplifying every aspect of song production to make things speed up by 100x? i'm considering just releasing a truckload of songs that are just keyboard + vocals, but i'm not convinced these would feel... "complete" enough. like, i still want these things to really rock. so i think i need a hybrid approach, like just having 8 tracks across the entire song or something. but i worry this just opens the door to things taking longer again. if i'm just recording room audio, there literally isn't a way it could take longer to produce than just..... the time it takes to record 1 good take. but then i feel like i'm just cheating and releasing demos no one's gonna wanna listen to. opinions would be appreciated, thanks
133639.answ: you're probably going to spend your entire life sorting this out. this debate never goes away. unfortunately there is no single answer, but, fortunately every answer is the right answer. 133685.ques: Hey bill. I can write a chorus but I have a hard time writing a verse. My verse melodies feel like filler. I want the verse to be as good as the chorus. Not sure what good means. Should the verse be catchy like the chorus? Hasn't worked thus far. Should I approach the verse differently?
133685.answ: first of all, it's almost impossible to write backwards. if you have a 'chorus' but you need something to come before it and you dont have it yet, it's almost impossible to find it, you kind of have to get lucky. BUT, a wise real estate agent once told me, "dont make the verse too good, you wanna save that for the chorus". And i wanted to disagree with him at the time, but i do believe that strategy should lead you to constructive places. Actually here's something i always almost forget to suggest: Just run the chorus and see what you wanna do after the chorus. then just continue from there. and if your chorus is good enough, chances are you'll end up leading back to it again. Then, take that part after the chorus, and start your song there with some alternate lyrics and it should lead to the chorus just fine. BUT, all this babble about 'verse' and 'chorus', it makes me want to give a reminder: the duck expert who thought all animals had beaks never discovered any of nature's wonderful beakless offerings. sorry for answering this question 4 times at once, but you can see i have a lot to say on this

133766.ques: do you think some people have a more natural inclination towards creativity (in any medium) that is at least a little bit innate? as someone whose education path and hobbies leaned heavily towards "maths/computers" and other stereotypically "logical" stuff i found myself drawn to, i have long realised that i don't find any of that deeply fulfilling but worry i have too much of a stereotypically "left-brain" to actually have any unique or interesting creative expression. then again i realise this way of overthinking it is probably part of the issue. i do have very specific tastes and know exactly what i would want to do, but for some reason feel frozen and unable to free myself from this thinking. like it always leads me to believe something is fundamentally wrong with me when i see so many people express themselves creatively so effortlessly regardless of medium, especially when said person is also someone who does computer stuff and is somehow able to be skilled in two totally at-odds modes of thought so i guess the crux of my question is how do i let go of this? or am i doomed? if i were a 6 year old child with absolutely no care for anything, it would be so easy
133766.answ: there's a lot to unpack here. you have very severe self-doubt built into you. this condition is of course common among artists, but it's something you probably just have to live with, for the most part. it also gives you huge powers because you care such a huge amount. you also mention you might be "overthinking", but again that's probably a common condition for artists, and probably something you'll have to live with, AND again, also a tremendous asset for creativity. if you can survive the damage it does to you on the bad days. Next, i have to point out a giant leap of reasoning you made that i dont think is fair: you say "so many people express themselves creatively so effortlessly regardless of medium". i see no reason to assume that. the confusion and turmoil you've described in yourself is easily a standard level of turmoil that artists can go through. Can be less, can be even more, but your assumption that it's such a breeze for everyone, that's way off. it's way way way way off. sure, at the moment the best stuff happens, that is a magic moment miracle breeze, but this is not without 1000x more moments striving, stressing, doubting, etc. or just waiting patiently for the clouds to clear, as if by magic. Anyway, the point is you've done the thing where you've assumed that it's "so easy" for everyone else, it is certainly not, and in many many many cases it looks extremely similar to the struggles you've described in yourself. The next thing i must point out that i think is really interesting is, you say "am i doomed". The person who likes cake and doesnt get to eat cake might assume they are "doomed" if they cannot eat cake. The person who does not like cake and is forced to eat it might assume they are "doomed" if they are forced to eat cake. So when you contemplate being "doomed", just by saying what this means youve already spilled the beans on what you want here. you want to be an artist, and if you 'cant' then that would feel like a deep failure. Not everybody even wants to be an artist. and they certainly wouldnt consider it 'doom' if they couldnt be. So just the fact that you are here thinking youre doomed if you cant do art, that tells you everything you need to know about who you are, and in a broad sense there is no need to worry about not becoming who you are. it means everything to you and you'll do it just fine, in your own way. and you did not convince me that there are any show-stopping obstacles preventing it. just wholesome challenges that you'll probably even feel thankful for later

133815.ques: i have a persistent issue with creating things: i will get a thing like 90% done, i.e. the point where i can say to myself "okay, i am mostly happy with this thing, i just need to clean it up a bit and add a little more spice before i can show it off to people", but then i just...don't do any of that, and i move on to making a brand new thing. the end result is a collection of things that make me Mostly happy but also vaguely guilty because i know i should be finishing them instead of making new things which i know i probably won't finish either. in my case the creating is just for fun so there is no external incentive to finish these things, but i would very much like to finish them regardless. is this an issue common among artists or am i just weird? and whether i am weird or normal, do you have any advice on Actually Finishing Things?
133815.answ: if you want to finish them you should set a time limit on these 'finishings'. because the ideal amount of work remaining is actually very little. and in many cases the best choice is to just declare it finished as is. A funny thing about real audiences: they actually often enjoy it more if it's 10% unfinished. it lets them use their imagination more, and it keeps them paying attention. On the other hand, to hear something 100% 'done' often feels alienating to an audience. there's the feeling it doesnt really care about their input

The bad news is, there's no such thing as a song in a cage. therefore there's no cage (that's the good news), which ALSO means, that your notion of 'trying to write a song' is NOT what you think it is. So go ahead and start writing the most not-songest not-song you could imagine. do precisely what you think would get you KICKED OUT of song school immediately

133837.ques: I often find myself trying to write a question to you, but struggling to find the words. (I made sure to just write this in one go for the sake of getting better and not going on a tangent. It feels aggravating to do though, because there are probably better ways I can express this.)
133837.answ: confidence is like the weather. you can't really predict it. 133847.ques: What's your favorite definition of love in all the possible definitions and explanations? How do you explain love to someone in a way that will make them understand what the feeling is like when they've never felt that way before? How do they know it's true?
133847.answ: there's a lot to unpack here. you are starting with a word and then trying to work backwards to what happened that that word describes. but for this i think it probably works the other way. something special happens, and THEN someone might identify 'ah yes, that was love'. but the joy in this is that you never know what form it will be in next. so it's better usually if you try not to predict at all. like dont even guess what it's going to be called

133849.ques: why can I never get started on making the things I have a passion for, and do you have any tips on how to hesitate less?
133849.answ: usually this is exactly what happens when you dont know the exact next step. which usually means the next step is asking yourself some key questions about what the next step might be, or what you'd like it to be. Like the actual, most tiny first next step, knowing exactly what that will be. The weird thing is, as soon as you figure that out, it happens, instantly, like you blink and you've done it. but i'm getting ahead of myself i dont want to spoil it for you

133898.ques: in the context of n, how large does a number need to be to be a "big number"? e.g. in the context of 500, is 200 a large amount? And if so, where does that line stop? I've heard arguments for n/2 or n/3 rounded up to the nearest 10, and I've even thought about n/e. And if there's no definitive line, how then do our brains consider numbers 'big'? This is a topic I'm very interested in, please answer
133898.answ: you can see even with such a simple and concrete concept as this, it still falls to mush. but i think i can get a handle on the mechanism here. 'big' is comparable to something. but it's not always said. so that's where it gets tricky, because people dont always say what it's comparing it too. and then 'by how much' is also a threshhold that is not mentioned, but it's "enough for you to care". you can see how wishy washy this is, imagine how insane it gets when you try to quantify how 'good' a song is

134107.ques: do you remember the last time you fell over?
134107.answ: sure this was pretty good. it was about 10 years ago. i was carrying a fresh slice of pizza in a pizza box, and a take-out iced coffee with a peanut butter cookie (in a bag) from the coffee shop. it was winter, in the city. the sidewalk was very icy. i was aware of this. Also this particular sidewalk had a slight sideways slope to it. I was also aware of this. so i was walking very carefully. Anyway, despite walking carefully, i suddenly took a spectacular airborne slip, feet just fly up into the air and i'm in the air for a full .75 seconds probably and i land perfectly flat square on my back. (which surprisingly left no injury and barely any pain, only just a small bruise in the direct center of the back, nothing else). the pizza and cookie were fine but i lost the drink, had to go back and get another one. i told them what happened, they were very impressed

134156.ques: Hi Mr. Wurtz, I caught in a Scary Pockets track you were on that you use trad grip on the kit. I had to learn both but have always preferred matched. Exposing my bias, I'm a little down on trad because a lot of drumlines I've taught use it for the snares because it looks cool (which it does, admittedly), rather than thinking of the development of the student, where matched is more applicable to other instruments like the keys or timpani. Do you use it because it's what's comfortable, do you switch between the two depending on the song, do you use it because it's cool (a good reason for any individual, but not for a teacher to use exclusively unless the students have the desire to learn it imo), or for some other purpose (and if so, what)? Also big props from a random stranger here, you're crazy in the pocket and the dryness of the kit on those tracks really accentuates that. And the ghost notes? The Groove? Don't even get me started. The reason I wanted to ask you, as opposed to an even more random stranger, is because I respect you as an accomplished and skilled drummer and would appreciate any insight you have!
134156.answ: disagree. most instruments the hands do something different that compliment each other and its very romantic in that way, many people would argue the origin was i think more practical than this, marching snares used to be slanted. But, my origin with it is totally random; basketball injury around age 10, then temporarily could not bend my left middle finger. so i switched. BUT, i was already hooked on dave weckl as a youngster and FOR SURE it looked cool, but i never had the motivation to switch (it's naturally weird at first). then that basketball fell on my finger, and i was like 'huh, i can still actually play if i switch to trad, might as well try it' and when my finger got better (maybe a week later?), as they say....... "you never go back." (well at least a lot of people say that). Ok but so, as far as "which one is better", i will firmly stand behind freedom of choice. you'll be just fine either way. matched is totally good, it obviously makes sense, trad was created for obsolete technical circumstances, so it makes perfect sense that that one will fade away. BUT, here's the funny thing..... if you ask around, it always seems like everyone who does use trad grip........ LOVES it, and they say it's so much more inspiring and feels much more elegant to have your hands doing different things, and it feels like the hands are responding to one another 'organically'. people really fall in love with traditional grip. Like the manual stick-shifter in an car, this really seems to be one of those "never go back" things. it's something people really fall in love with

134159.ques: Does it ever happen that you have a song idea that you think has potential, but for some reason you just can't get to work; it just doesn't "click" with you for whatever reason? 134159.answ: the dirty truth is that almost every song idea feels like this at some point. so i actually have a pretty much religious devotion to the art of 'rescuing' song ideas. Even in a hypothetical rare case where a song is so 'good' and 'right' that it doesnt need to be 'rescued', it then IMMEDIATELY gets cursed because the expectation becomes so high it collapses the confidence of the whole endeavor. and then you need to rescue those by actually making them worse. it's hard to explain, songwriting is actually ridiculous

134243.ques: are you still a piece of garbage all these years later? if its not a binary yes or no answer please give an excruciatingly detailed status report on your garbage-ness
134243.answ: as i always say, this was written based on a journal entry from 2007. the journal entry never said anything about 'garbage', but this was just my interpretation of it in 2015 as i was doing this particular jingle writing exercise. so in fact, doing the jingle was a great way of redeeming myself. But actually what we're talking about is the video from the day before the one you mentioned. "i'm a piece of garbage". Then, as the exercise goes, i randomly select another journal entry, and by sheer coincidence ANOTHER one from... like the next day (in 2007) came up. just as garbagey. after pondering for a moment what i'm gonna do about it, whether to skip it or whatever, as usual i realized the golden rule of creativity 'theres always a way' and i wrote a second jingle "STILL a piece of garbage" as a sequel. Is it true what they say about sequels always being less popular than the original? not in this case

134244.ques: how do i get better at writing chord progressions?
134244.answ: what do you think the chords are about? Hint: sing the words and melody, or, if it's for a filmscore, watch the scenes. if neither of those things exist yet, then usually dont do the chords first. Or if you do, dont think of "writing chords" when there's no melody words or scene yet. sure you can start with some chords, but only insofar as it gets you in the mood to start writing the actual thing youre writing (the words/melody/scene, what happens). Because without that, the chords will be complaining to you that their existence is meaningless. Chords are there to inject emotion subliminally into things. Without any 'thing' there to inject emotion into, chords cant even do their job. Like a hypnotizer beam with no one to hypnotize. I mean this is like at the dinner table, when you salt your plate first, and cant figure out which way to do it. salt the food not the plate. are you getting it

134245.answ: no one knows, that's one of the dark arts

134256.ques: Hi Bill! Would you please talk about your thought process for writing drum and percussion parts? I love what they add to your songs, and as a non-drummer, I have little insight into them.
134256.answ: for the most part, creating music is not a 'thought process.' In fact, as soon as you start having one of those, the music vanishes and everything turns to crap. So the whole game of music writing is to just basically wait for yourself to totally forget you were trying to do anything, and then the pure imagination takes over and goes totally wild. Then, you catch yourself having fun and then you start to try to 'think' about how to continue trying having fun which of course ruins it, and then you have to wait again until you forget about it again so it can come alive again. More specifically you asked about 'writing drum/perc' and so for that i have to say the same thing i said to the 'writing chords' person from a few things down, which is i'd say dont write those things, write the song. i mean, as a songwriter, if you find yourself 'thinking'.... about 'writing'... a 'drum part',..... its all over. better off taking a nap and then at least you might dream something

134364.ques: i've been trying to use your factoring method. i'm doing well on a number i was given online, but when i added 39 to turn 3471 into 3510, and killed the zero, i then got 13x27, which is taking me further. i couldn't find in the note you made that has the intructions on how to factor a number what to do if that works, since you ended up with a prime number. whats the deal?
134364.answ: i'd say i think you missed 3. you start by checking 2, then 3, then 5, then 7 etc. and when you get a hit, divide it out. so in this case you'd divide the 3471 by 3, get 1157, check 3 again (you always have to check the same factor again after a hit because it could have multiple, but you never have to check past factors again). so then you continue on to check 7, 11, etc. Then you'd hit with 13, you'd have seen that probably by adding 13 to get to 1170, so 117, and you'd hit it that way. so 13 is in. Then you divide the 13 out, 1157 div by 13 is 89, and now 89 is the new 'big number' to continue with, or to be your answer, depending on your level of experience. theoretically if you didnt know 89 you would continue, i guess you'd have to check 13 again. but you actually dont anyway because 13 squared is already way bigger but if you knew that then you probably also knew 89 was prime anyway. but anyway, so then you just have to collect your results to get the primefactorization of the original number. 89x13x3

134372.ques: do you ever feel like you compare yourself to others in an EXCESSIVELY unhealthy amount, like you see others make things that are better than yours and they get more love for it compared to you and your stuff? ive been feeling this way recently in this game where i make stuff and theres like a really huge community surrounded by this type of stuff, but a part of me is kinda wanting to distance from the community while also wanting to be in it, like just a year or two ago id make a lot but nowadays if i wanna make something i get afraid of not making something as great as my previous stuff, this is probably really vague lol
134372.answ: no, i dont do it all. perhaps that's a whole different problem i'm not too worried about it. When i was very young i suppose i started to think most of the most popular stuff out there wasnt nearly good enough to deserve its popularity, but i've had many many years to get over that and be much more forgiving, and now i dont even notice it anymore. i'm just not worried about it. heck, take a lays potato chip and sell it and get a hit. see if i care. doesnt stop anything better from being an even bigger hit. the potato chip was probably just the in-between thing. actually now that i think about it, a potato chip sounds like a pretty solid idea anyway yes this is one of the ways we all tense up, from time to time. creativity comes from a mysterious place. you can't really control it. all you know is that it usually never wants to visit you until you've, eventually, calmed down. it doesnt really seem to care HOW you calm down, but it generally is never gonna show up until you do. the good news is, it WILL show up (eventually). last resort you will just exhaust yourself and fall asleep and it will be there in your dreams

134386.ques: how do people decide on bpm's
134386.answ: the best way to clock the tempo of a song youre hearing in your imagination, is with a 'tap' feature on a metronome. you tap a button along with the beat. dont look at the machine, just get in the zone and start vibing to your tune, then when youre comfortable start tapping along to the beat (on the 'tap tempo' button), then after you've settled into the pocket for a couple bars, open your eyes and look at what tempo it's saying. then that's your tempo

134425.ques: You say you now do prime factorization with "way bigger numbers" than you used to. Can you provide an example of one of these numbers? Thank you very much.
134425.answ: the standard is 9 digits, if i'm randomly selecting one for myself. but it's usually more fun if it's actually a number that i've seen somewhere, on a serial number or something. dollar bill serials work great. it gives the number just that extra hint of realness. That being said, if you go with 9 digits, AND it's high (starts with 9, let's say), AND it turns out to be prime, then it would probably take (just crunching the numbers here.....) years to figure it out. But if it does break down and has some factors, then it could be done in just a few minutes. the more prime it is, the longer it takes. for bedtime i always choose a 9 digit, because if i choose a 6 it's fairly likely that i will finish it and have to get up and get another one. the 9 digit is almost guaranteed to not be finishable before falling asleep. For example: i just random-selected a random 6 digit number (709456) to test this theory, exactly 7 minutes ago i selected it and now i have solved it (139,29,11,2,2,2,2). that was a fairly lucky selection because it halved its way down to 44341 almost immediately, and then i basically only had to check up to 29. honestly the hardest part was at the end i had to re-count how many times i halved it

134460.ques: What's the real difference between thesauruses and dictionaries? Also, are words' meanings simply other words, or do the meanings exist in a separate plane of existence?
134460.answ: thesauri are more about giving you lists of single words you can replace for the original word. but the dictionary doesnt really care about that, it just describes the word in any number of words. the meaning exists seperately, and the only way to get a good idea of the meaning is to hear the word used in real life, in many different contexts by different people. and the more you hear it the more you will get a feel for what the word means, even if you could never accurately describe it

134528.ques: What does being a freak mean too you? And would you consider yourself a freak? Why or why not?
134528.answ: it's hard to know what i would use the word for until it comes up. it's a loose word, describing things generally in the neighborhood of weird or crazy, but has no exact definition. also can be used for positive or negative meaning, depending on the context. for example "i'm a freak for [something]" sometimes is a way of saying you're really into that thing, you like it, you're crazy about it. Totally other context that word finds itself in, is the expression "control freak" which just means you insist on having a lot of control over what you're doing. freak is not the rarest word in the world, and it shows up in a lot of different places usually with the help of other words to give it meaning

134529.ques: what does it all mean (in a generic sense)

They say Dr. Evil (the bad guy, you know, from the movies) the backstory is he was originally created out of love, but due to a lifetime of trauma he became Dr. Evil. as you say, "originated from love but isnt actually love itself". 134607.ques: Why is the volume of a cone not 1/2 a cylinder?
134607.answ: i suppose you are imagining a side-view rectangle, with a triangle shaped cone silhouette drawn on it. and then spinning that around 360deg to make the volume. But what's happening is the parts near the middle get their volume increased less because they make less big of a spin. If you flip it, fill in the opposite part instead, more of the area is toward the edges, so when it spins around those parts create more volume when they are near the edges and have further to spin. at least that is my first intuition. honestly i had never thought about this before until you said it and it actually shocked me at first

Technologies to speed things up or make things predictable, truths about the universe replacing one another in quick succession, works produced with intelligent consideration and careful hands and painstaking attention to detail and precious time, things getting better, things getting worse; the Giza Necropolis will outlive them all. 134737.ques: (2x2, 2+2 question) hmm, I'm not sure if I'm getting the chart, can you explain a bit more for me?
134737.answ: the question was "wow, why are 2x2 and 2+2 the same". and i feel the same way about this issue. i looked into this just a few weeks ago. very important question and i'm surprised i never took it more seriously before. what you wanna do is graph it to see what's going on. graph "xy=x+y" and on top of that graph "x=y". the first graph shows you all pairs of numbers whose sum and product are the same, and the second graph draws a line through at the points where the two numbers are the same (like 2 and 2). you immediately see this also works for zero. 0+0 = 0x0

134866.ques: If you had to write the pseudocode for how you turn perception into music, what would those lines be, and what part of you gets lost in that compression?
134866.answ: sometimes the 'compression' youre talking about actually helps, because the main spiritual 'payload' here is happening outside the lines anyway. so sometimes it can end up being actually more 'potent' at lower detail levels. The magic ingredient to music boils down to just knowing the person is really there and cares (even if they care about not caring, that can work too). as we hone tighter and tighter into that, we see that actually by definition there can be no exact rule set for this. it boils down to what can be described as 'unpredictable meaningfulness'. In the search for a 'code' such as youre doing, it's almost like you're playing a game of cat and mouse whereby you narrow in on perhaps the most vital technique in creative expression, which is defying whatever guidelines youre existing in. Think about how you'd prove to your best friend that you're really you and not an impostor
