137787.ques: when others cannot understand what is true perfection, such as the so called coaches, what is the true meaning of being perfect in a lap, what is a true meaning of racing in itself, is it for pride? is it for the sense of victory and ego? or is it because it is sometimes the greatest metaphor of what life is and was and will be? is perfection achievable that way? can i control this car in a way that is a part of me and win everything? or is there something or someone controlling my destiny or is it me? is it me who can control what i do and when i do it for my own reasons? is the day going to end my way or the other persons way? what is life in that way? is it because evolution increased so much we have created new atheltes way more talented than its predecessors? are former greats better or worse than current greats? are humans truly doing better than what we were doing? is a competition the true meaning for what it means to be human? to win a championship or an event, to become the best, to be atthe top of whatever mountain one might want to go to, is driving supposed to be perfection or pride or even both? what is the meaning of it
137787.answ: it seems this is about creation, and then the development of those creations. For example: a game is created. Wow cool! a game was created, now everyone wants to see if we can find the best ways to play it, best ways to win at it, etc. For another example: improvised acting, let's say. someone comes up with a really cool idea or pattern, Wow! a really cool idea or pattern! now everyone wants to try to find new ways to extrapolate from that idea or pattern. For another example, someone makes a brand new meme format. Wow! new meme format! everyone tries to see how many things they can do with it. For another example.... being a human. Wow! can run and jump, do things, have opposable thumbs, Okay, now, let's see how good we can get at working within those settings. and usually, i almost forgot to mention, part of each game can be making new games within the game. for example let's say being human is the game, well within that game is all the sports and athletic games you mentioned. Heck you can have a video game within a video game also. Anyway, hopefully i have shed some light on this, it seems almost everything is about creating games, then playing and developing things within them. if you start to feel too trapped, that's the incentive for creating new games. if it feels like there's too many new games and you feel lost, that's incentive for sticking to one game and developing and improving as much as you can within that one game. hopefully this ties in constructively amongst some of the metaphors and questions you are working with

137818.ques: Does any form of art have an inherent higher value than another? Does a movie mean more than a poem by its scale and grandeur? A song vs a painting? If so, is there a Highest form? A pinnacle of artistic integrity that every other kind fails to match? I write frequently, but my works will never be as appreciated as a film. Is this by design?
137818.answ: it's basically an illusion, but, the prestige that i think you are noticing is something that comes with time. you'll notice that these prestige formats are always the older ones, which were also looked downupon once upon a time. in fact, just yesterday i was listening to a film director from yesteryear talking about how films back then were not very well respected as art, until television came along, and then the filmmakers were very excited because now they have 'something to look down on'. this cat and mouse chase continues on through the ages, and it can be quite amusing to follow

137842.ques: How do you write lyrics or dialogue good without sounding pretentious? I feel like an obvious answer might be "well that's what you think. who cares what people think." ME! I care what people think! If I put a thing out into the world and everyone thinks it's kinda cringe and pretentious then I will feel bad! Do you also feel these things? I guess it really depends on what you actually want, I guess. If you wanna sell your art as a product then different things matter than if you want to just get validation for it or if you just want to create something for your own sake. Sigh. I dunno if I'm answering all my own questions here I don't know. what do you think
137842.answ: suppose you deeply care what other people will think, and there's no reason why you wouldn't, in fact the only recipe you have for judging that is what you yourself think, and feel. in fact, most (all) of the most cringe content is a direct result of this guessing game, everyone guessing what someone else is going to like. but getting back to the first thing you asked, i never found out if you do or dont think your own work is pretentious. your question seems to stop just shy of revealing this important detail for me

137843.ques: can instrumental become the lyrics once you run out of things to say with language?
137843.answ: sure. in fact, a standard and powerful maneuver, across all kinds of music, is this: sing some words, play a riff. sing some words, play a riff. Or the other way around, play a riff, sing some words. etc. The words will explain what the riff is about, and the riff will demonstrate how to feel about what the words said

137900.ques: Why do you think our brains enjoy the harmonic intervals that we do? A perfect fifth sounds so nice it's almost nothing, but a 2nd interval sounds discordant and tense. It seems like our brains enjoy frequencies that nest inside each other neatly. Do you think it's some fundamental mathematical principle of our universe that any neural network that forms out of the matter inside it will gravitate towards neatly nesting frequency combos like fifths and octaves, while sensing other closer intervals as more chaotic sounding? Or do you think to some other species, a 2nd or 9th could sound perfectly resolved while a fifth sounds discordant? I ate the weed oil out of my weed pen and now I think I'm stuck on one of those "do we all see colors differently" types of unanswerable questions. I'd love to read your thoughts though.
137900.answ: here's what seems to be happening: in abscence of any tangible definition of what's good or not good or what anything exactly means, it becomes an irresistible miracle when someone still manages to communicate nonetheless. Like a pile of paperclips. there is no definition for the rules of communicating with paperclips, but then you watch an artist come along and boom they communicate something to you with paperclips. like 'how did they do that', and it may feel like a total miracle, which it is, but in the broader picture it's not surprising that this works. it's just impossible to explain

137916.ques: I'm writing up a concept for a video game and its surrounding world/story/etc from my head onto a big text document. Do you think describing its music in terms of a genre + country + decade (i.e. "90s Japanese soul" or "2000s sambass") is a good thing to do, or should I focus on describing the particular sounds/instruments/feelings when I imagine the game's songs instead? The game's world won't contain our countries, decades or any human history or humans at all, so I don't want to describe it in a way that makes any eventual musicians overly worry about anachronisms and real-world counterparts. Or is describing music in text the wrong way to go overall? Also, are you aware of any goth-subculture music with flutes and/or very prominent bass? One of my planned characters would probably play this music/these instruments as a hobby, and I'm not sure if it exists yet
137916.answ: do anything you can to describe something. ideally the only way to describe something is by showing them the actual thing. but it sounds like you are looking to collaborate, so you'll need to communicate with them about what you think youre going for. grab onto anything you can. when collaborative professionals communicate to each other about what they're thinking about for their movie, on their song, whatever they're working on, they're surely constantly referencing things that already exist, while also making it clear that none of these could be a perfect description because youre attempting something to describe something that doesnt exist yet. so it's usually a combination of multiple different references. like this element from this movie, combined with that element from that book, etc etc. or like "what if this character from this movie was in this other situation instead". or "its like this but without the that" etc etc. these are the kind of conversations that you'll hear among professional creative collaborators, almost ubiquitously, across the board

137967.ques: I've got two questions for the price of one (but I marked it up 200% right before asking, art of the deal baby) 1. When you write chord progressions, do you think of them as overlapping individual melodies that should stay cohesive, or do you just hear each chord as it's own thing? Like in a C Amin G F progression, you can think of t first E as starting a melody that goes e-c-b-a, or the first G note going g-e-d-c. Do you hear these individual melodies and try to keep them coherent, or do you just feel the texture of the chord? 2. Can a melody or chord progression function the same way in any key? I don't have perfect pitch, so I tend to just hear the intervals between notes, and a fifth feels the same to me whether it's A and E or G and D. The context around the intervals tends to be what makes them sound a certain way. I tend to choose a key based on where my voicings are at to avoid things sounding muddy, but if I shift my entire song one semitone down it doesn't usually make a difference to me (unless I listened to it one semitone up first and it's fresh in my mind).
137967.answ: "when you write a sentence, do you think of it like overlapping individual words that should stay cohesive, or do you just hear each word as its own thing?" the reason i ask you this is because you must be familiar with sentnces, both written and spoken. so you must have some experience in this. and i promise you the answer for music is qualitatively the same i know ive beaten this analogy like a dead horse, but i'm dreadfully serious with it. "do you hear individual letters in a word and try to keep them coherent, or do you just feel the texture of the letter" hopefully by now youre thinking something like "i have no idea how the words come to me, i just speak. and im usually thinking about what im saying, not about the words and certainly much less the letters" yes, this is good. this is normal. this means you are hearing music like music. some children become cursed when they learn to memorize all the notes cold. then they're stuck hearing music non-musically. they dont even hear chords and melodies, they just hear individual notes, and they often cant even hear multiple ones at a time. where as with 'musical' hearing such as you have, you'll be able to instantly recognize pretty much anything a reasonable song can throw at you, in most circumstances it sounds like youve got the hang of this. the bad news is now youre cursed with knowing how much of a weird and misunderstood question "do you have perfect pitch" is

138089.ques: 12.30.25 8:31 am Power chords are a mystery to me. I don't understand why anyone would want to remove the flavor that the 3rd gives. Doesn't the chord sound kind of empty without it?
138089.answ: for example: Why would you want to carry only 2 things in a bag instead of 3? arent the 3 things light enough? and the answer: not if they are heavier things. So, in terms of music, you've probably been thinking of chords approximately in the middle-C range or higher. but if you want to put chords in a lower range, you find out very quickly that if you play triads (C E G for C major) it starts to sound cruddy and incoherent in this range. so below a certain range, it sounds better with only the root and 5th. And, by the same exact phenomenon, if you go even lower, it starts to sound cruddy even with the 5th so you have to take that out too. This is why the 'bass' only plays one note. but if they ever play two notes they go up a little higher and play root and 5th. i find, as a general rule of thumb, if you find the 'lowest' point youd ever want to play a C major triad (for example), then go down one octave and that will usually correspond to the lowest a power chord would work, and if you go one octave below that you find the lowest point that single notes work. also i should mention when guitarists kick the distortion on, for 'metal' music, they use a lot of power chords not just because they are playing those metal riffs in that mid-low range, but also because the distortion increases the clash of the 3rds, and particularly exploits the 'power' that the root and 5th has. I'd almost dare say if you play root-5th on a gentle piano in a high octave, i honestly wouldnt even think to call that a 'power chord'. power-chord i associate with that low-ish (not quite bass but too low for triads) range, and also with 'metal' music where most of the riffs are played in that range, with distortion on. i hope that answers your question

138210.ques: When you're an artist, nothing is off-limits. Video gamemaking, board gamemaking, conlangs, worldbuilding, etc., everything that we're supposed to look down on as "not real art" is fair game for sandbox exploration just as much as "real art" (e.g. songwriting, embroidery, cinematography, and so on). Am I on to something?
138210.answ: sure except i dont think the public opinion of the definition of 'real art' is even as well defined as you think it is. For example, if you had listed all 7 of those things in the same set, and asked me which ones do i think are 'supposed' to be considered 'real art', i wouldnt necessarily sort them out in the same ways you did. in general it's common for people to be slow in accepting newer forms of art. so generally when people say something is 'not real art', they are usually talking about something newer than whatever else they have in mind as 'real art'. in fact, just the other day i was listening to a film director from yesteryear talking about how films back then were not very well respected as art, until television came along, and then the filmmakers were very excited because now they have 'something to look down on'. this cat and mouse chase continues on through the ages, and it can be quite amusing to follow

138217.ques: I'm noticing a lot of questions on here along the lines of "Hey Bill what chord is [sequence of notes]" and then you'll respond with something like "F#sus4add7" (or probably something that makes more sense). I know you usually say that studying/using "music theory" is not required/important when making music, but how necessary/helpful is it to identify these chords when making music? Does knowing these chords help with making better-sounding music, or is it mainly just a party trick?
138217.answ: neither. it can be a useful way of writing down chords. never entirely accurate, because even with the exact same notes there's still a million different ways to play something, but, it's similar to the way we can use the written word "word" to correspond to the sound your voice makes when you say the word "word", while still not describing the actual exact way you said it

138220.ques: What would the staging/visuals of a theoretical Bill Wurtz live performance look like?
138220.answ: people on stage in a band playing songs. this goes without saying, but, NOT trying to mimick exactly a recording. just playing the song genuinely, afresh. they've also done a million live shows before, so their chemistry and experience together is very strong. no backing tracks. no click tracks. no projected video. small enough venue that you dont need to mic the kick. these are generally my guidelines for how i'd prefer to do a live show. also a strong preference for never switching venues, playing in the same venue every time. there may or may not be multiple elements of this that would have a hard time coinciding with actual reality, for various reasons. which may or may not be why you tend to not see me doing live shows. each time i describe these preferences, people usually take it to mean, 'ah yes, so you are going to do a live show'. which is why it's important to clarify that i probably will not. it would be nice, and i'm very jealous of people who do, but my psychological problems probably run way too deep to allow me to make sense of it. i have a hard enough time just producing audio/video

138238.ques: what's the best way to write a brigde in a song? and what's its purpose?
138238.answ: defining what the heck a bridge is is a lot like trying to hold a pile of sand in your hand. If you are in a position where you think you are 'supposed' to make a 'bridge', you will have 2 problems. 1: 'bridge' will be undefinable, and 2: you wont have any feeling that you need it. Another way to say this: it's a lot like when youve been sitting in a chair for a certin amount of time and you just need to stretch. and you say, "what's the best way to stretch, and what's it's purpose"..... no idea. no need to worry about or think about it. if you feel like stretching, you'll stretch and you'll automatically know how. To re-iterate what i'm trying to say another way: the worst thing you can do for a 'bridge' is to think it is 1 official thing that is supposed to happen. the only thing that really defines it is that it's something different, something unexpected. so if you try to pin down what it's supposed to be, that's a one-way ticket to bridge-failure, which usually occurs when people try to put a bridge because they think theyre supposed to, when it actually doesnt feel right. Maybe a broader way to look at this problem is, "what do you do after 2 full rounds of the main form of the song". and the answer is drastically different in every song, and often you dont even need to do anything. in general the most common problem is to be out of touch with what it actually feels like to have heard the song all the way up to this point

138243.ques: What was Paul thinking when he wrote blackbird?
138243.answ: for most songwriters, and i'm almost certain paul mccartney would be included in this, if you start to 'think' while songwriting youre screwed and you wont get anything good. so i would assume that a song like that would be what comes out when you forget to think. in fact, paul often says (and many other songwriters say the same thing which i will paraphrase here), "i dont know where these things come from, and honestly i wouldnt wanna know because that might spoil the magic"

138244.ques: It seems like with how prolific you have been in the past and how hard you are working on your projects now that you have never really struggled coming up with ideas but instead you always move forward into the next project. Is the time you have spent away been filled with stretches of time without knowing what to do or has it been spent mostly trying to figure out how to develop existing ideas? Also, I would suspect that with the amount of time that has passed one would eventually get tired with the idea that made you realize that your absence would be a prolonged one because of how long it would have taken and how the mind naturally wonders into new things. Was there a moment that made you realize that it was going to be a while or have you forgotten?
138244.answ: someone about 5 questions down has a similar problem to me. the overload of ideas literally clogs the system. every day of my life for at least the past 20 years i've been living under the looming threat that it may be completely impossible to unclog the system. it's a miracle i've ever gotten anything through around april 2023 i decided to try a new selection strategy that i've always wanted to try, because i just couldnt take the terminal fear and anxiety i've always had with the selection process. the strategy was to select as many things as i want. i ended up choosing 10 (i was actually surprised that i didnt choose more). So it was pretty obvious that that would take a hideously long time, and the truth is i was obviously running from something. so deep down on some level i probably knew that i was living in a fantasy world. and i've been running from the consequences all this time. just facing up to them now, and despite how scary it is i'm starting to get the warm eager feelings i got in feb 2014, or aug 2018, or feb 2022, etc. Which was fully predictable also. The solutions this time wont be quite the same as last time, because there were reasons why the past solutions eventually failed and were untenable, but i'm very excited nonetheless, mostly because i'm ultimately pretty confident in my ability to solve problems. And in fact, the 'clog' phenomenon actually builds up a monsterously strong desire to explode. You'd think this wouldn't still be effective after the fact that i've already gotten a lot of stuff out over the years, but in fact it feels stronger each time. there is the vividly unshakable feeling that the majority of my best work has yet to come out

138257.answ: i would certainly argue not to think of it that way at all. i dont agree with calling it a 'progression', because that creates the implication that it is supposed to 'progress', when quite often it doesnt feel that way. problem 2: if you are thinking about only the chords then you're usually shooting your songwriting in the foot, in my opinion. problem 3: insisting on making your chords totally unique from any other song is almost like insisting on your new car having a different number of wheels than any other car before

The issue is that if they have somehow transformed into another plain of existence that exists outside of time, it would be pretty hard to write about that since everything is unable to be described. And language fails because I would think if an individual somehow transformed and found themselves in a space after death, their environment would be so radically different that their thoughts would be unrecognizable from human thoughts since they would occupy a different form entirely. So is there any way to write this that I haven't thought of?
138281.answ: the only thing i can suggest, and you may have already done this, but you just gotta write a million things and then you just get good at writing, and then you can pretty much trust yourelf to be able to handle anything. things that cant be done? heck those are some of the easiest things to write, because that places you instantly in a land of miracles by default

138286.ques: Whenever i am doing something e.g making music, coding, writing on paper and a lot of other things, i get really nervous and anxious about doing things perfectly. And so i begin either: - a searching rabbit hole of "should i do X or Y", "should i use { in a new line or not?", "should i write using lined paper or blank", "should i use pen or pencil for math", trying to seek an objective truth of some sorts or looking for someone with experience to give their own opinion i can follow - a deep freeze of uncertainty on my decisions, "should i come up with my own chord progressions or kinda steal this one", "should i listen to x y z music or not", "should i use this notebook for thoughts or ideas or drawing", "should i make my keyboard auto correct and auto capitalize and suggest our turn it all off for authenticity" And so where i wanted to get at is, with all that ive stated, is that i feel like i am so alone with these issues. It feels like no one understands this specific type of insecurity in everyday life, and so i came here to ask you because you might have advice for me: Have you dealt with something similar to these problems before, and would you consider it really a "problem"? Should i embrace this as a part of myself even when i realize it takes so much of my time i could spend doing stuff and not just obsessing over every little detail? (here i am asking should i do X or Y questions again) Could this be good for me somehow, and be part of why i feel like i have a "good taste" for things in general, but little work of my own to show for it?

138357.ques: I've got no experience making music. Spent a few decades wishing I knew how to, but kind of assuming I'd missed my window to learn, and that me not knowing all the formal language around compositon was an insurmountable barrier. Thank you for contributing to me unlearning that! I'm giving it a try now. Since I have no instruments and no money, I'm starting with a sequencer. It's a sequencer with deliberately limited options, which has been helping me to not get overwhelmed. I have enough understanding of notes and octaves just from cultural osmosis that I'm able to kind of guess at what combination of notes will make the sounds I want, and feel out a couple adjustments with trial and error. It's felt good so far! But I'm hitting a wall now. I'm struggling to connect any of what I create the way I see it connecting in my head. Individual parts ("phrases," I think?) come out the way I envision them, but something's getting lost in translation in the space between them. Massaging out mistranslations in the individual parts is trial and error, but how do I feel out the connective tissue? It feels much more abstract. My gut instinct was that it's similar to how you weave a thread between sentences in English to connect them, but something's missing in that approach. I'm also finding I have absolutely no natural sense for writing rhythm instruments at all, and suspect that's playing a big part. As though I'm envisioning a rhythm I can't clearly hear, and thus never comes out right. It's just this formless energy humming in the background, but I feel certain there's something tangible within that. How do you learn to give form to rhythm?
138357.answ: to hammer the language analogy again and again, it's the same as learning to communicate in a language. so there's good news and bad news: the good news is it happens automatically as long as you just keep doing it and enjoying it. the bad news is of course it takes a really really really long time. How long did it take you to become comfortable in english, from your absolute starting point to the point where you could conversate comfortably?

138401.ques: give me ur best music advice mr music man
138401.answ: it can influence people's feelings about what else it is that they are currently seeing or hearing. its powerful effects are often attributed to those other things. for example, a horse makes an entrance. you say 'wow, what a wonderful and powerful horse!' when asked if you noticed the music that was playing to match the horse's entrance, you might say 'no i didnt notice the music but i noticed how freakin powerful the horse seemed. its a powerful horse isnt it', when perhaps in actuality it may have been just an average horse but the music subliminally made you feel it was powerful. this is a common tale

138423.ques: by the time I get home, even after feeling rested, I cant seem to work on anything at all. do you have any advice for this?
138423.answ: here's what i would do, but i have no idea if it will work for you. do a brainstorm, either in text or in audio, and just think out loud about what you wish you could do, without any pressure to do it today. this might be more enjoyable at first than actually working on something. in the course of a brainstorm, you may get some clarity about exactly what steps you might want to take, then you can schedule an exact time for tomorrow (preferably the same time as when youre brainstorming, but 24 hours later) to do a very specific step. then after doing it (the next day), brainstorm a little more to evaluate where you are, and make the next plan for the next day. mountains are moved this way

138424.ques: Do you find it offensive when someone calls you talented, as if they're suggesting you were born with your abilities, as opposed to having developed your skills through years of dedicated work? I think some people take being called 'talented' as an insult and I'm wondering if you feel the same. Since learning about this, I hesitate calling anyone talented, so as not to offend.
138424.answ: i dont think anyone thinks it means your born with the abilities, but it might mean you were born with a tendency to learn new abilities, or, let me re-state that, a tendency to ENJOY learning new abilities. so if that's your idea of a good time, to just let 10 hours blow by working on your latest tricks, ..... in fact this can be alienating. you get used to all your friends not caring about these obsessions youre into, and you drift apart. So then it can be uplifting to be recognized as 'talented' because then at least it feels like the end result is valued, even if they never actually wanna play with you

138489.ques: do you think human lives are pre-determined? Or is that just some nonsense... like was I supposed to make my best music in the future before I even started making music or did I just make it because I am a very creative person and I love creating and I love music and making music . And was I supposed to ask this question on your site at this moment instead of working on/doing something else? Do you get what I mean? What do you think?
138489.answ: let's say there are a very very very large amount of predetermined possibilities. let's also say that the number of those possibilities is infinite. but then how are the choices made? sometimes a choice is made by doing this thing called 'thinking' where you imagine multiple different futures. so in fact the need to choose between multiple options, that is what gives rise to our conscious experience. huge disclaimer this is just my opinion

138525.ques: What's the craziest coincidence you've ever experienced?
138525.answ: i did a very short video once called "what if spain was in greece", not knowing very much about spain or greece, i just picked those names because they fit in the jingle. Later i realized that the landscape for spain and greece look extremely similar. THEN, i noticed that the Spanish and Greek languages, phonetically, sound EXTREMELY similar. their phonetic inventory is exactly the same, and even spanish speakers say greek sounds like spanish with nonsense words, and greek people say the same about spanish

138526.ques: just watched a video of a guy analysing your music and explaining all the key changes and chord progressions etc, and now i wonder how much you actually think about that stuff while writing a song? or, as you've previously mentioned i think, do you just write it without really thinking and then it just happens to be 'wow look its a chord progression that people use'
138526.answ: "i once saw a video of a guy analysing all the muscle and joint movements our legs make when we walk. when you walk, do you think about all these muscle movements? or is it just a huge coincidence that you happen to walk with legitimate muscle movements?" hopefully you see what i'm getting at here, the answer is hopefully pretty much neither. yes you know what youre doing, no you dont have to think about walking, in fact you're probably thinking more about what youre saying while walking, or what youre seeing or where youre going

138532.ques: I've tried to break away from 12 tone equal temperament, but I just can't come up with anything better. Can you come up with a better system? Things that bother me about 12-TET are : the sharp major 3rd in a triad and the slightly wide leading tone in a melody. Simple triads sound best with Just Intonation and melodies sound best with a scale notes derived from Pythagorean Tuning. (Wide tones and narrow semitones) But even then, 12-TET is the best compromise for chords and melodies, not to mention the consistency across different keys. Any tuning systems I could come up with is worse. 12-TET is the most practical it seems... Is it time to accept it as is?
138532.answ: when i went down this road, my conclusions were not that 12-tet is a 'compromise', but it's something way way way way way way way better than that, which to be honest i didnt expect, based on what we're generally told about it. may i refer you to the excrutiatingly long and overly emotional telling of the results of my research i gave once on here

138549.ques: since when do you know that you need to do things to get better at them? i'm asking because this seemingly obvious fact took me very long to realize, partly due to numerous textbooks, pieces of documentation, and educational materials that reinforce the unconscious belief that you know how to do things once you've read an explanation about it. I wonder if it'd be good for these materials to state things more explicitely, or if it's obvious to most people and i'm looking for a scapegoat to avoid taking responsibility for my general incompetence
138549.answ: i dont know where the idea comes from that an instruction is sufficient for being able to actually do something. i think it probably comes from a general overestimation for how accurate language is. i really started realizing this actually when i started learning foreign languages. and then all these memories come flooding back to me from early childhood as i remember learning english for the first time. and then also it becomes impossible to avoid the analogy to 'walking.' thats why i always go around saying 'did you learn to walk by being told how'.... and also reminding people 'and by the way how long did it take'

138560.ques: I was driving earlier and saw a road sign that said SLOW. At first I was like, ok, maybe they just didnt want to put in a different speed limit sign for a new, lower speed limit. But they already put in the SLOW sign, so it wasnt just a matter of not wanting to put up a different speed limit sign. How fast is SLOW? It was on a road with a 30mph speed limit... so like 5mph? 10? 15? And then there was no second sign to say "YOU CAN GO FASTER NOW", so how far am I supposed to drive slower than the speed limit?
138560.answ: the SLOW sign is a suggestion, not a law. the SPEED LIMIT sign is a law. Sometimes you see things that contradict each other. For example, you might see School Zone 20mph, and then right next to it "Low Speed Vehicles Prohibited". i happen to know the second sign means no tractors, but the two signs were probably not designed with the intention of co-existing near each other. Signage, like pinecones, are not perfect

138646.ques: bro i had a disappointing ass dinner. the server kept hyping us up abt how the food uses so many spices therefore it's super flavorful. Tell me WHERE were the spices when i got my entree because that shit was BLAND AS FUCK & the whole appeal of the restaurant was that it's supposed to be flavorful moroccan cuisine. is the flavor in the room with us because it must still be in morocco. have you had a similar experience recently?? What would you do in this situation??
138646.answ: there is something particularly humorous about something being hyped way up and then actually being the opposite of what it's hyped to be. this reminds me many many years ago at burger thing, when they first introduced the "chicken fries" (or maybe one of the many times the re-introduced them), they come in a tall, top-heavy box. the box is somehow designed so it has a sauce holder on top. a little slot where you can put the sauce container. They had arrows pointing to it and then had a description "Sauce goes here. Yup, we promoted the guy who invented it." whether or not they actually promoted someone, i dont know or care, but here's the thing: the sauce fits fine in the slot, but as soon as you start taking the fries out, to eat them, the box immediately falls over. it only works when the box is full of chicken fries. this brilliant invention immediately fails as soon as you start using it. What would have seemed mildly silly, became an even more humorous spectacle based on the hype story written on the box. i'm also thinking of debut of the tesla cybertruck, where they demonstrated the unbreakable windows by hitting it with a sledge hammer and the window immediately broke "what you do in this situation" is you collect stories like this for amusement

138651.ques: you know what always fascinated me? how chord inversions can sometimes completely change the feel despite it still being the same chord...
138651.answ: i mean it's like whether you put the cow on the right side of the barn or the left, in your farm picture. but i think in time you will stop calling them 'inversions' because there is really no 'default' order. actually they way they're most often used in music, chances are, will be in a different order from the 'default' order you may have learned. the truth is, if the page says "C", that doesnt say it all. either the writer or arranger or just you the player with good judgement, will have to decide which octaves to play those notes in. To use a woodworking analogy, the more wood you work the better a woodworker you will be, and thus become better at working with wood

138681.ques: Recently I realized that I had forgotten some lessons that I learned many years ago, and that I needed to teach myself those same lessons again. Forgetting those lessons had begun to negatively impact my life. Have you ever had to do something similar? If so, how did you handle it? I was thinking about how to prevent it from happening again - perhaps writing some things down to reference later. But if I did that, I wasn't sure what was important enough to include, or inversely, what didn't need to be written down at all. I wondered if there was a different approach I hadn't thought of.
138681.answ: you can get into this game of organizing all your wisdom and everything you know, but ultimately youre not going to prevent the ebb and flow of different realizations, different mentalities, different strategies, etc. It's nice to write things down and compare notes, but you can't promise yourself to uphold every mindset all the time

138720.ques: in "slow down" one of the lines that's stuck with me the most is "it's easy to feel if you're useless, but it's strange when you tell your friends", can you describe the backstory behind that line?
138720.answ: yes, it's sucks. it's the worst line in the song. There are all kinds of benefits and drawbacks to being on a firmly imposed 2-week production deadline. Sure, a lot of things sound fresher and more frisky in a great way when working on a schedule, but then at the same time there are certain parts that just suffer simply because you didnt have enough time to wait for a more refreshed mood to come around, so youre forced to write stuff when youre not feeling it. so you sometimes get crap like that line there

138742.ques: after several days of absolutely NOTHING working while i tried to design my puzzle game, i finally got somewhere. literally just 3 new puzzles that i'm happy with. it feels so refreshing to explore aconcept and actually find new things. for so long i was trying to run awayfrom my current puzzle mechanic ideas because i hated them, but that only made me hate everything else i found too. so just now i tried to actually focus in on what i already had and see if i could find more there. and you better believe it, i did! but... that sense of discovery and progress is already fading. i REALLY don't want to go back to the horrible place of hating all my ideas and not knowing what to do or where to go. i know you say it's a process of being uninspired, then getting super inspired, then being uninspired again, etc. but right now it's like i'm spending almost all my time in the uninspired place with only small bursts of inspiration... i feel cheated. i'm getting this feeling that i have to grab onto the moments of discovery because they keep going away too soon. i'm just tired of spending so much time being stuck.
138742.answ: i'll let you know if i find something that actually works for this. i've been searching for a little while now

138900.ques: have you watched nirvanna the band the show the movie? if you have, what'd you think of it?
138900.answ: these guys have an amazing filmmaking strategy that relies deeply on improv and editing, and then assembling it into a coherent story in a way that in always feels like a magic trick, keeping you wondering which came first, the writing or the action (and sometimes the answer is not what you think). Unfortunately what starts to ruin this wonderful game is the encroachment of ai. not the use of ai per se, but the encroaching thought of "they could/should have" used it, starts to ruin the amazing game that this team has with their filmmaking method. also one of the strongest points about the nirvanna-the-band content is that the piano playing always matches, and it's actually quite tragic to see jay's hands not matching the sound, giving first time viewers the impression that this is just another movie with a non-piano player playing the piano, which of course it's not. it seems clear to me that what happened is he's probably playing back to the future stuff but they couldnt clear it. so that is a major unfortunate weakness of the movie. generally it's quite strong, perhaps about as strong as the viceland series

138902.ques: This morning I had a dream that mocked my aspirations for self improvement and progress. It felt like such a condescending confirmation my mind had conjured up just to discourage me from what I wish to accomplish. "The mire is your home and your home it shall stay." Im immensely aggravated by it. Because I know very well I can do something to remove myself from this situation. Even if only a little. I put myself in it after all! I think what it is, is I've grown too accustomed to disappointment. I'm afraid to go after the things I really love. Really want. Ironically it's precisely chasing after these things that would propel me out of the mud now hardened cement. Why am I allowing my nightmares to prove themselves right? It has no right at all to foretell anything.
138902.answ: maybe it's bringing you to a point of clarity about it, where 'no' you dont have to obey the dream logic

138938.ques: So the videos from the multi-select process are just toast? There's no way that's true. You can finish what you started
138938.answ: "2 friends" was done in about 2 weeks. "perfect" was done in maybe 3 or 4 weeks. this is encouraging to think about. at this stage i have 11 videos at approx 90% done. so they will be finished and released one at a time, but with new song/videos in between which will hopefully feel more appropriate to me in the current time. and then continuing on with at least 100 more new song/videos. if i sound like i'm telling tall tales, believe me i've been doing that all my life. so i have proof that something eventually sometimes works dont i

138939.ques: In 987 there is an incredible moment. "Your home was never like that" the chords you go to on "like that" are so beautiful and I really didn't expect them at all. Within the musical context of that part of the song how did you choose to use those chords? I never would've thought of that and would really appreciate some wisedom. Thank you Bill. Huge fan.
138939.answ: i think it's just a lot of experience doing songs with chords. it's not really that different from what it does at the end of the other similar parts, but it's maybe the equivalent of a behind the back throw. Honestly what that part sounds like to me is, i was feeling stuck in that same melody and basically just repetitions of that same section, and i was feeling restless, so i was getting 'differenty' with the chords to try to compensate. but really i probably would have been more satisfied if i had taken it in a whole different direction there somehow rather than just fitting more stanzas into the same box of a B section earlier established. in fact i feel very similarly about this as to a line that was brought up just the other day, in slow down, where i was also surely feeling restless and dissatisfied with just piling additional stanzas onto the same A and B sections round and round. in both cases the writing was crunched for time on a self imposed emergency schedule as a last resort attempt to save myself from myself

138991.ques: When you get inspired to make a song, what's the first thing you do?
138991.answ: it doesnt really work like that. let me see if i can explain: "when you get inspired to take a breath of air, what's the first thing you do"? it's already known that i'd like to please take hundreds and hundreds of breaths of air, preferably more. the only question is, what are the songs gonna be like, how are they gonna go, and so actually the first thing to do when it's time to produce the next song is choose which one of my songs i'm gonna do. Or perhaps youre asking what's the first thing i do to come up with new ideas for songs. but that's almost never the same time that i go ahead and produce or even finish writing that song. perhaps it should be, but it's not. i go guerilla with my new idea writing, then i go guerilla with middle writing, then from there as soon as i finish a video/song, i already know i'm gonna do another one, that's not a question. the question is which one am i gonna do, and if the writing's not finished then my next question is ok how does it go so far, and then i try to imagine what the next part goes like

138992.ques: What's going on in the everybody wants to rule the world drum part?
138992.answ: you have a triplet feel (you can say it's 4/4 with triplets, or you can say it's 12/8 with eight notes, or even 6/8 depending on how big you want the bars to be on paper), you have 4-on-the-floor, which means the kick drum is hitting all 4 beats, you have snare on 2 and 4 ('backbeat'), and then perhaps what you want to know most is the percussion, there is a percussion accent happening on alternating triplets. which makes a cool pattern when you accent one out of every 2 notes when the notes are grouped in groups of 3. so if you're counting "1 trip let 2 trip let 3 trip let 4 trip let", the percussion accents (the louder notes in the tambourine or shaker or whatever) will occur on "one TRIP let TWO trip LET three TRIP let FOUR trip LET". at least this is what i recall happening. i havent really listened to the song since i saw it in the credits for marty surpreme in the cinema, but the song has been around for decades and i've heard it plenty, and to my best recollection that is what the drums and perc are doing. and the snare will give you some nice fills at the end of the 8 or 16 bar segments, usually "BAP BAP _ BAP BAP BAP" in the last two beats (or the last 6 subdivisions). hopefully my language is more or less intelligible to you. i'm trying to be broad in terms of catering the different kinds of musical or mathematical literacy you may be working with

139025.ques: How do you approach perfectionism? Sometimes I keep working on a song because it just doesn't sound quite right, but I can't quite tell what's wrong with it. I'm trying to teach myself that the unintentional parts of a song, like the parts I didn't really deliberately make that way but just kind of became that way because I couldn't figure them out, are just as uniquely mine, and thus beautiful as anything else I could write. Do you have any kind of similar attitude when trying to become happy with a song that you just can't get "perfect"?
139025.answ: perfect is poison anyway, but the good news is it doesnt even exit. so it's like imaginary poison. i CERTAINLY would never pursue this imaginary poison on purpose. but i catch myself reflexively going for it by total accident. sort of like a compulsive twitch. and one needs to certainly slap oneself on the wrist here, because it usually is a sign you are suffocating in an ocd spiral leading to nowhere and need to come up for air

139043.ques: In an interview you expressed distaste for people saying your name again in the middle of talking, then you said "there's nothing wrong with it until you think about it" - can you elaborate on this? I think I know exactly what you mean but want to hear your thoughts on why it's weird/unsettling
139043.answ: it makes them sound like a sales person, like they are selling something. it's just a classic trick that salespeople do, to think they're being more personable but it certainly has the opposite effect. friends dont do this when it's just 1 on 1, especially in the middle of a conversation. the reason it might be used is to distinguish between multiple people. you are in a room with 5 people, youre having a group discussion, you say "so Bob, i wanted to ask about this...." that makes sense, because you are letting the room know which person you are talking to, but in a 1-on-1 conversation this is useless. and people who do it in a 1-on-1 convo immediately sound very sleezy, to me

139050.ques: regarding your answer to 4.13.26 12:52 pm , wow. I've always found it hard to finish a song after a long time of not working on it, when i try to it always feels as if I'm locked out of having the ability to access the "magic" (if i had to specify i guess i'd call it thematic consistency, maybe even climate) Oftentimes it seems clearly to me that the old part and new part are like unmatching puzzle pieces Any advice to combat this? The unability to 'continue' the song organically i mean; i agree that sometimes two distinct parts, even not particularly fitting each other can be nice, but i've been searching for ways to make them fit thansk
139050.answ: yes yes, the great trick here is you can now write about your current situation. heck, start writing about what you remember it being like to have written the previous part. write the story of how you feel now, and what that bit does or does not mean to you now. i use this trick over and over again, and it's maybe the most fun part about songwriting. especially because you thought you were screwed but then you made it meaningful, even more meaningful than it otherwise would have been. The thing that the 'songwriting gods' are always trying to hammer into us which we (as songwriters) ALWAYS keep forgetting: how do YOU ACTUALLLY feel NOW. what does this actually mean to you NOW

139075.ques: how do i get the hang of chord extensions. im pretty familiar with how regular old triad chords get along with each other but i have no intuition of how chord extensions happen
139075.answ: i regret the fact that the 'academic literature' has led you astray. my advice is, pretend you never heard anyone say 'chord extensions'. the thing they are actually referring to is not anything extended. i mean, think of it this way, have you ever heard one of these things in music? if you havent, then why do you think you need it? just because you hear people talking about it? i mean, what are we listening to anyway, music or people talking about music? I will wait patiently until you hear a song and you ask me 'WHOA, WHATS THAT CHORD THERE?' and then i can be like, "it's these notes in order: bass C, and then in mid-range Bb D Eb G", and it's called "C minor 9", that's just what it's called. and then we can debate the logic of the naming convention from there, but if youve never noticed it naturally in music then there's no reason you should really be curious about it, unless you just want to say "chord extension". which you could just start saying now if you really want to

139079.ques: how exactly does one write lyrics that rhyme while also conveying frustration, confusion, and romantic difficulty (in the sense of "she doesn't know i exist") and still not sounding like a clichéd fuckin cornball? i ask because i want to music but i'm also bad at rhymes because i intentionally avoid them in my speech and have therefore never gotten good at using them
139079.answ: 1. write while singing the lines at the same time. 2. dont try to rhyme. "How exactly do you grow a round orange"? that's just how they grow. if you start making things up and singing them, actually you'll find that they will be free to be timed in a way where they dont even have to rhyme, because they wont be like "line, line, line, line". you'll just be saying stuff musically. and then from time to time rhymes may happen automatically. i think the world is kinda brainwashed into thinking a certain way about rhymes, or thinking about rhymes at all, in an unhealthy way for songwriting. Including the fact that when it does happen, what sounds the best is actually just when the vowel is the same but the end consonant is different anyway. but youre seriously shooting yourself in the foot if you try to rhyme on purpose. it's a curse for sure

139128.ques: I apparently write very close to an AI and I don't want to. It served me just fine most of my life but in this new world of non-human entities everywhere I want to develop a writing style of my own to set me apart. Bill, do you have any tips on figuring out a new writing style?

139151.ques: Okay. So recently I was having a conversation with a friend, and he presented his theory that some people in the world aren't "real". And although that goes against everything I've come to believe (HOPE that I believe??), I can't get it out of my head. I'd have brushed it off before, but now when I talk to someone & they say something that sounds like no thought was behind it - entirely empty/ devoid of internal logic, I'm reminded of my friend's theory. Of course I'm only thinking of it on a metaphorical level, but I'm wondering what it is that makes some people appear this way and I cannot prove they're real. ??? Do you know what he means??
139151.answ: from an artist's perspective, i can tell you about a feeling that may be similar to this, but not quite the same. when you spend most of your days dreaming around in la la land, thinking about new and different creative ways to do things, say things, think things, etc etc etc, then from time to time someone comes along who SEEMS to you like maybe they are not so ridiculous, and they just kind of say or do it the normal way.... and there may be a bit of a feeling of 'oh, how boring'. But it doesnt mean theyre not real. it just means they've put their focus into other things. there's plenty of things that i probably do boringlyer than them

139176.ques: what is the story of when you made "La de da de da de da de day oh"?
139176.answ: ok so in 2009 i finally got fed up with the fact that i didnt seem to be writing any songs despite the fact that i felt like i had more than enough skills to be able to do so. so as a last resort i deeply downgraded my standards and just started scribbling garbage on paper. i wrote a beginning about 'i dont have enough time to say the things i want to say' etc, something very much resembling the first verse you'd recognize from that song. (but also not quite the same). that song lived only on paper and also didnt have the 'la de da' chorus, it did something else instead and ended after about a minute and a half. fast foward to 2012 i finally got fed up with how much songwriting i had done without knowing how to organize it or choose which ones to work on. so i built a master list of everything i had, in order of when it was written, and then i started working on each one. the "i dont have enough time" bit came up, and i fixed the verse, and wrote a chorus (the "la de da" chorus that you would recognize). fast forward to 2017, i'd figured out how to finish and produce songs, but i was really fed up with how long it took. i was trying out a 2-week schedule, alternating between song and not-song, always with a video, alternating whether or not i appear on camera in the video. it SEEMED to work ok with a non-song video called 'soap tips', and next would be to do a song video with me appearing in it. i should have known that this would be impossible in 2 weeks but i tried it anyway. on the selection process i landed on the 'la de da' song, which still hadnt been updated since 2012, so it had just 1 verse and chorus written. BUT.... the day after selection, i started doubting the selection. i actually wanted to switch to a different song. then i went to get a haircut and i was thinking about the selection, as thinking about the 'la de da' song again, and then for some reason just at the moment i was getting into the chair, (songwriting loves to work like this) the first 3 lines of the second verse just rammed itself into my head. so with that momentum i figured ok that's the selection, then at home i finished writing the rest. then i recorded it, made the video, etc, and it all took way way way way way way longer than 2 weeks

139177.answ: so crazy it just might work

139181.ques: i guess im a big fat liar because i like to say i just make art for myself and other people's opinions are whatever but really im horrified by ai because it can convincingly duplicate all the things i pour real emotion into and the only defense i can offer for my own work is "well i actually did put emotion into it" which is true but no one really cares about because the end result is the same to them. any tips on being less of a big fat liar?
139181.answ: well, this issue can of course be maximum confusing for everyone, at least for now while we get used to it and learn to understand what it is, what it's not, and what it means and doesnt mean. However, it's not nearly as bad as you think, and in some ways it's absolutely wonderful. let me just disect one of your points here: you say no one cares because the end result is the same..... Even if that's true, imagine if the quantity was cranked up to a trillion. whatever becomes overflowingly abundant becomes de-valued. so now no one cares about the fake version either, just because there is SO much of it, it becomes worthless. now, true, also, that that also means that it devalues any kind of thing that SOUNDS LIKE something the machines would cough up, and that may be considered unfortunate for human people who were making.... lets say 'normalish' music before, .... but the effect that this has on everyone is it cultivates a huge thirst for something new and different. whatever it is that the machines cant cough up, that's what will be desired. this has always been true, but now it's accelerated. yes, people will be using the machines in all kinds of ways up down and sideways, in fantastically creative ways (which perhaps older people will not quite understand), and then at the same time there will also be some people that just choose not to use it, and they reap some pretty crazy advantages from NOT using it. in many ways this is analogous to the invention of synths, the invention of drum machines, the invention of SAMPLING ("effortless, he just stole someone elses song and rapped over it!"), PITCH-CORRECTED VOCALS ("but she cant even sing, shes not a real singer" etc), and so on. All these may be horrifying concepts at first, but the advantages are very genuine, AND the benefits of NOT using them are also genuine. AND, also true, the major thing that really truly moves the needle is CONNECTION with audiences. so as long as your audience is human, they are going to care deeply about the human presence in the art. Difficult to prove at times, yes, this may be. but that is also a very fun and interesting game that will become EXTREMELY satisfying to win, for both the artist and the audience. that being said, i will not be using it at all, for the forseeable future. similar to my decision not to use loops, and not to use any pitch correction. this is certainly not to frown on anyone who does, it's just that i have some very interesting skills in terms of songwriting and playing, which can be used very much to my advantage. The key, of course, is to steer it clear of the generic. For weirdo artists like me, for example, it's a license to not be ashamed of being weird and different. If there ever was any pressure to "try to sound commercial" or "try to sound like what's popular", this new technology will SHATTER that bland obligation, better than i think anything else ever could. being a sentient ass human being is a priceless commodity in art, and its value is now thrust to center stage more than it ever has been before, as any and all mechanical aspects are trivialized and removed from the equation. as usual it reminds me of almost every major invention of yester year

139199.ques: do you have guidelines for when it's best to finish writing a song and when it's best to leave it partially written for later you?
139199.answ: if i come up with something that i know is going to get me really excited next time around when i see it again, that's when i stop. However, what can happen is, i put it away.... and then i'm minding my own business and then the song will still be on my mind and then i will accidentally come up with the next part. so i come rushing back to add it. then i put it away. then it's still on my mind and then the next part comes to me. so sometimes songs are just so excited to get written that it just happens. but otherwise, as long as i have something that i know will really excite me next time, i put it away

139235.ques: have you ever thought about how many horse-related metaphors and idioms there are, like im so hungry i could eat a horse, get off your high horse, beating a dead horse, never look a gift horse in the mouth, you can lead a horse to water but you cant make it drink, from the horse's mouth, healthy as a horse, stubborn as a mule (half-horse), and so on
139235.answ: it's a fascinating quality of language, that it can encode our history. "full steam ahead" ... "get on the band wagon" .... "cream of the crop" .... more history references, baked into to our language expressions still in use today. heck, even single words, like 'rewind', can tell us about how we used to record on tape

139237.ques: Is there any reason why you made the final key for "and the day goes on" F# instead of F?(referring to your idea catching video.) by the way, it's such a fun, happy song, and it's a great choice for a morning montage for the main character of a movie
139237.answ: when it comes time to record the song for real, you check to see what key you wanna sing it in. check to see if the high parts are too high, or the low parts are too low. see where it feels best. most songs get raised a little from where they were written, because i was writing it at the keyboard singing casually in mumble mode

139238.ques: I just noticed in your songs page, i'm a princess has the date as 4.27.19, even though the video was released to Youtube on March 23rd, 2021. Does that mean the song itself was complete but you were still working on the video during that time? If that was the case, why did you choose to complete here comes the sun before i'm a princess?
139238.answ: the song was done on 4.27.19. Then for the video, which i started immediately after, i slipped and fell DEEP into a rabbit hole of "3D animation". i had already done bits of 3D in almost every video, but for this one i wanted to try doing the whole thing in 3D graphics. so yeah i slipped and fell into a rabbit hole and changed the whole system. Then when i was finally ready to do videos again, i still had half a video (well, 25% of a video) for princess that was from the old system. so this one has to be a 'frankenstein' with the first 25% being in a different world than the remaining 75% (as you can probably see.) I decided i would rather make a fully new video for my first video on the new system before attempting a frankenstein. this is a good reminder to myself actually, that these frankensteins can and will eventually get done (i have surely more frankensteins to this day)

139260.ques: i'm a competent drummer but i want to get better at playing bass and guitar. I'm ok at that too, and am working on functional ear training and singing. i'm trying to learn the bass for long long journey. i'm getting it but very hard for me to memorize it all. idk... maybe the answer is in the song title my question is with the whole music universe of things to learn, do you think there are common traps that people learning music fall into? i feel like i can't decide on what and how much time to put into a specific thing to learn anyway, thank you
139260.answ: in this case if youre memorizing then you are DEFINITELY on the wrong track. for example, i have NO IDEA from memory what exactly the bass plays on that song, in terms of exact notes, but i could play it again just fine, and it would be just as good but with different exact notes sure, memorization, for example i am assuming that you are doing this music stuff because you fell in love with music at some point. so that love will guide you to what you want to do. since i told you earlier what NOT to do on bass, i suppose i have to tell you what i think you should do instead..... it's probably a good idea to just start playing the bass along to songs. you can mute the bass track on the song (nowadays you can do this in a million ways, but probably the easiest way is to just play the track through your phone speaker without headphones. poof, no bass!) and play your own bass through amplification so you can hear your bass but not the song's bass. and that's it, you'll figure out what you need to do. the other thing i would suggest is definitely dont choose artists who have a habit of being complicated like me

139302.ques: i've made an online document full of really comprehensive analyses of a game that i love, but every time i try to turn it into a script for a youtube video, i hate it. something about the way the information is presented on the "page" feels so much nicer to me. it honestly might just be my complete inexperience with turning written words into spoken words. i really want to make it into a video. what can i do? maybe practice with smaller projects and build up to it?
139302.answ: if you ask me (and oops you did), i generally can't stand to hear people reading a script. What a hypocrite i am! because most of my videos are scripted exactly. Unless you are a great actor, again, what a hypocrite i am, because i am not exactly an actor. but i'll tell what i crave, what i thirst for desperately, in video content these days, when someone has to present information. i wanna see a human ass being, real person, on camera, holding a physical piece of paper that generally has the information you wanna say, and youre just telling the information. youre just hangin out being a real person not reading an exact script, just talking about the information and looking at the paper as necessary. and if they tell me to like and subscribe or whats 'coming up' later then i punch a hole in the screen usually that being said, this is a kind of skill that you get better at with practice

139349.ques: So you record all the different parts to your song...but how do you make them sound like one sound? (In the mix) all my parts sounds so seperate, any mixing advice I guess? You're my favourite musician by far. Thanks
139349.answ: i can probably spill the beans on this one because it's very old technology, probably been around since the 1930s and everyone's been using it ever since, on everything, but especially on radios and broadcasting and in retail stores. it's called "dynamic range compression" and it levels off the volume, making the loud parts quieter and the quiet parts louder. applying this in various ways is basically how you get all your musicians strapped together like they're coming out of the same box. the devil is in the details of course, but for the problem you suggested, 'compressor' is the main device used for this. the only confusing thing is the title. 'compressor' might make you think it has something to do with files or data but it's a totally different thing same name sorry about the confusion in that regard

139409.ques: bill, sometimes i get this feeling that my art should be created in complete isolation from all external actors, to let my ideas out in their "purest" form, even though it's impossible. i imagine most artists have dealt with this. we live in a human-curated world. my instruments were assembled by someone else, from materials manufactured and processed by someone else, built for a temperament designed by someone else, and so on. our own creativity is inexorably tied to the work of others. so i guess my question is, do you create music despite this fact, do you mentally embrace the interconnection, or some other third thing?

139417.ques: hi Bill. In a literature class in high school, there was an assignment to choose a song that meant a lot to you and to analyze it. I chose 'just did a bad thing'. I was wondering what that song means to You? (note: I dont think there is a "right" or "wrong" way to interpret a song; everyone interprets art differently based on their own experiences and I think that's beautiful. But nonetheless, I'm still curious about how much our takes differ)
139417.answ: the 2 things most memorable to me, which i always say in response to this, are things relating to the selection process, and the production schedule. for the selection process it's my main example of the explicit failure of over thinking. i had it down to about 20 choices for which song to choose, and then one by one i ruled out each one "no, not this one" "no, not this one" "no, not this one" etc, and then i had ruled them all out. so in a fit of rage, i uncancelled all of those semifinalists and on a total whim picked one that was probably my least favorite, on purpose (it only had the first line so far, 'just did a bad thing' and just seemed totally weak and useless, despite the fact that it was a semifinalist.) As soon as i started writing on it, i realized that this was a great choice, because in fact when you have something weak to work with, it ignites the creative engines with more power. and i noticed this within the first 16 bars or so, i said 'wow, i feel more alive now because im forced to compensate for a weak first line'. maybe in fact the 'cancel' line is coming from the fact that the other 'better' songs were cancelled. anyway, the other thing notable for me about this song has to do with the production schedule, because i had recently fallen off the 2 week schedule and was kind of dangling by a thread, in shambles and tears. but it was done in 4 weeks instead of two. which is much quicker than it usually is without a schedule, because i'd just been on the habit of doing it on schedule so i was still relatively quick, but it was slower than a schedule. so sometimes it feels like a 'sweet spot', as i sometimes say, i accidentally worked on it for too long and not long enough, which inadvertantly was maybe just the right amount of time

139442.ques: how do you learn music so you can think in it like a language? i am too old to a native speaker of it now. do i just learn it like usual with theory first and try express ideas in it and fail and try again until it's serviceable? or would theory be putting the cart before the horse? where the horse is playing an instrument rather than speech and theory is the writing you learn later after the sounds.. is language even an adequate analogy for music or is it far beyond something as limiting as words thank yiu
139442.answ: the language analogy is of course my standard analogy. if you try to 'study' the mechanics before doing it, it wont make any sense to you. like learning 'grammar', think back to studying english grammar, at, let's say, age 8, for example. was that before you could speak, or after? it was after. if you tried to study english grammar before you could speak, it wouldnt make any sense. and it barely makes any difference on how you speak, because you already know how to use it without knowing you know and the other similarity in general to language learning is (and this may be actually a misconception about both), it's easy to learn but it takes years. some people mistake the length of time for difficulty. but learning is so easy that it would be impossible for you to actually not learn, given enough exposure and experience

139478.ques: BILL! How do you decide what to work on when there are a million things you want to work on? I have no idea how to prioritize anything
139478.answ: i keep a list of all the things, then i generally do 1 thing at a time. And to choose which one to do next, what often works best for me is a partially random selection. the pre selection i do on my own, i just go through the whole list and mark off all the ones that seem to 'excite' me, then IMMEDIATELY when that pre-list is done, send it to a random selection. (count them up and then tell the computer to give you a random number up to that number, and pick that one). the reason i say IMMEDIATELY is because if you spend any more time looking at the list, you may chicken out on all of them one by one

139544.ques: Are there any songs of yours in your catalogue where you didn't write all of the elements as one from the beginning (eg. lyrics)? If so, would you care to share which ones are those?
139544.answ: when writing, occasionally the melody/chords will try to get ahead of the lyrics. and as soon as this happens, the whole machine withers to a halt within seconds. however, the words are sometimes allowed to get ahead of the music by maybe a quarter of a second. you think of words and then a quarter of a second later you melodify it with chords etc. Also occasionally a fun exercise is to come up with titles. just a title that sounds cool, without any music. then melodify them later. Also, another way the words can get ahead is when theyre not exact. so for example, if the melody starts to keep getting ahead and everything is drying up, i remind myself to push back into the words realm and just go and brainstorm what i want the next part to talk about. and then i can paraphrase it musically. Also what can happen sometimes is the first idea for a song will be an entire section of melody (usually a chorus, or sometimes first verse). then later the words have to be found, which is usually a very tedious and long search process. so if i may attempt to summarize,...... the melody never gets more than 1 section ahead, and the words never get more than 1 line ahead. and in both of those cases it's almost always exclusively in the case of the first idea. and then generally as the songwriting moves forward, the melody has a tendency to TRY to get ahead, but when it does for more than 1 second it sucks the life out of everything and i have to remind myself to get the words back on board

139550.ques: ok, so i guess what it feels like when people use a compound word (usually a noun) when 2 separate words (usually verb + adjective) would have meant what they wanted to convey, it's kind of like when people write with no punctuation. i can follow it, but i have to slow down a bit and make sure i'm surmising meaning correctly. kind of like encountering a speed bump or pothole. no one else seems to notice, many people don't even know the difference. i guess that's fine. example: "break through addiction" vs "i'm addicted to breakthroughs." yes, i am still talking about this shit.
139550.answ: of the 2 items in quotes, i find the second one much more clear. Reading your original description of the problem, it sounds like you are saying you are favoring the other one, but that makes no sense to me. the second one seems to me by far more clear. in fact i often remove spaces from 2-word phrases when necessary to make them more clear. a habit i may have picked up from dutch. for example, "speedbump" is a candidate for this, surely. Especially the way you just used it, because you listed 2 items, and one of them was 1 word and the other one was 2. so it was 2 items in 3 words. so when reading it quickly, it looks kind of like 3 things. also it seems arbitrary why "speed bump" has a space and "pot hole" didnt. i say "speedbump or pothole" is much more clear, visually, as 2 items

139551.answ: even better than you were expected to be

139569.ques: would you rather go simplistic or extremely detailed on music making.
139569.answ: i'm always fantasizing about brutal simplicity. what i aspire to, cherish, and respect, is brutal simplicity. if there ever was any glory in complexity, those days are surely over. and i have very warm feelings about that turn of events, and i am fully on board with the power of simplicity. HOWEVER, my problem is, always, the complexity sneaks in through the side door. every single time. like when a person compulsively bites their fingernails without even realizing it, that's apparently what it's like with me and complexity. there is no glory in complexity but for some strange reason it's surprisingly hard for me to avoid. and the avoidence itself actually starts to become complex

139580.ques: Since the beginning of this year I've been going through an artistic block and I don't know how to break free. The classic "oh just make anything and you'll eventually get back on track" method isn't even working. I've created a handful of stuff during the block, but it doesn't really motivate me to do more. It's almost as if I'm stuck in quicksand and the more that I move (in this case create), the faster I'm sinking down. At the same time though, I don't think I could just go on a hiatus. If I did, all the skills that I had would begin to vanish. I've already noticed that the quality of my work has gotten sloppy, which makes me feel all the more discouraged to carry on. It's really annoying but I'm all out of ideas on what to do about it. Any tips?

139598.ques: why did you heat the porridge for the wrong amount of time
